text: In this work, we employ the term "syntactic" in a broad sense to denote a linguistically motivated, graph-based annotation of a text segment, where the resulting graph is directed and contains a path between any two nodes. While this typically refers to syntactic dependency trees or graphs—and indeed, our framework currently indexes Enhanced English Universal Dependency graphs (Nivre et al., 2016; Schuster and Manning, 2016)—the system is also compatible with more semantic annotation schemes (e.g., Oepen et al., 2015), provided that a sufficiently accurate parser is available for such representations.
source_text: In this paper, we very loosely use the term "syntactic" to refer to a linguistically motivated graph-based annotation over a piece of text, where the graph is directed and there is a path between any two nodes. While this usually implies syntactic dependency trees or graphs (and indeed, our system currently indexes Enhanced English Universal Dependency graphs(Nivre et al., 2016;Schuster and Manning, 2016)) the system can work also with more semantic annotation schemes e.g,(Oepen et al., 2015), given the availability of an accurate enough parser for them.

text: This paper introduces the task of Deep Question Generation (DQG), which involves generating questions that necessitate reasoning across multiple pieces of information within a passage. As illustrated in Figure 1(b), a deep question may require comparative reasoning over two disjoint segments of evidence. DQG presents three distinct challenges that traditional QG systems typically do not address. First, unlike generating questions from a single sentence, DQG requires document-level comprehension, which often entails modeling long-range dependencies in extensive passages. Second, selecting relevant contexts to formulate meaningful questions is non-trivial, as it involves identifying the relationships between disparate information fragments. Third, the system must ensure accurate reasoning over multiple pieces of information so that the generated question remains answerable based on the content of the passage.
source_text: In this paper, we propose the problem of Deep Question Generation (DQG), which aims to generate questions that require reasoning over multiple pieces of information in the passage. Figure 1 b) shows an example of deep question which requires a comparative reasoning over two disjoint pieces of evidences. DQG introduces three additional challenges that are not captured by traditional QG systems. First, unlike generating questions from a single sentence, DQG requires document-level understanding, which may introduce long-range dependencies when the passage is long. Second, we must be able to select relevant contexts to ask meaningful questions; this is non-trivial as it involves understanding the relation between disjoint pieces of information in the passage. Third, we need to ensure correct reasoning over multiple pieces of information so that the generated question is answerable by information in the passage.

text: Ultimately, chatbot evaluation metrics should be user-centric, as the primary purpose of these systems is to provide humans with satisfying experiences. Traditionally, self-reported ratings based on Likert scales have served as the de facto standard for dialogue evaluation. However, our analysis reveals that these ratings are often skewed (J-shaped), noisy, and insensitive due to inherent biases and variance across different users. We propose CMADE, a three-stage denoising pipeline designed to refine self-reported ratings while simultaneously constructing an automated, comparison-based dialogue quality predictor. CMADE demonstrates a high correlation with expert judgments on pairwise dialogue comparisons, achieving 89.2% agreement and a Cohen’s Kappa of 0.787. Table 5 illustrates an example of a dialogue with a noisy self-reported rating (Score-5) identified by Data Shapley: User: the whole harry potter series Sys: whoops i think i misheard you. can you repeat the movie name please? User: all the harry potter series Sys: ah, i don't know any movies by that name. sorry if i sometimes confuse you saying something else with a movie name ... ... Sys: oh, okay. anyways, i really enjoyed watching black panther and wonder woman recently. what's your favorite superhero movie? User: super man Sys: what was that? can you please say the movie name again? User: stop i gotta go.
source_text: The ultimate chatbot evaluation metric should be user-centric, as chatbots are there to provide human with an enjoyable experiences. Previously Likertscore based self-reported rating is the de-facto standard for current dialog evaluation . However, our analysis indicates that self-reported dialog ratings are skewed (J-shape), noisy and insensitive due to bias and variance among different users. We propose a three-stage denoising pipeline CMADE to reduce self-reported ratings and, at the same time, build an automatic comparison-based automatic dialog quality predictor. text: Conceptually, one might expect attention mechanisms to be less effective when applied to individual characters than to words, as character-level representations often lack the semantic density necessary for capturing meaningful sentence-wide dependencies. Contrary to this intuition, recent progress in language modeling (Al-Rfou et al., 2019) has shown that attention-based character modeling is surprisingly robust, raising important questions regarding the performance of Transformers in bilingual and multilingual translation at the character level and the specific architectures best suited for this objective. This paper is dedicated to investigating these issues.
source_text: Intuitively, attention as an operation might not be as meaningful for encoding individual characters as it is for words, because individual character representations might provide limited semantic information for learning meaningful relations on the sentence level. However, recent work on language modeling (Al-Rfou et al., 2019) has surprisingly shown that attention can be very effective for modeling characters, raising the question of how well the transformer would work on characterlevel bilingual and multilingual translation, and what architectures would be suitable for this task. These are the questions this paper sets out to investigate.

text: Natural language processing (NLP) is one of the most rapidly expanding fields within computational linguistics and artificial intelligence; for instance, the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL) saw a 140% increase in submissions between 2017 (1,419) and 2020 (3,429). Furthermore, over 4,000 pre-prints were published on arXiv in 2019 alone. Consequently, it has become increasingly challenging for researchers to remain current with the evolution of new methodologies. Presently, the standard approach for identifying relevant literature involves keyword searches on platforms such as Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar. While these search engines are effective at indexing vast quantities of research, they are limited in several respects: (1) they rely on traditional information retrieval techniques and often fail to process natural language queries effectively, such as "what are the effects of label smoothing?"; and (2) they are optimized for retrieving relevant documents based on titles and abstracts rather than providing direct answers to specific inquiries. Researchers frequently seek precise answers to technical questions, such as how to mitigate posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) or the costs associated with crowdsourced sentence labeling. With existing search engines, finding such information typically requires synthesizing content from multiple papers. Therefore, it is essential to develop more sophisticated tools that enable researchers to extract answers from scientific publications more efficiently.
source_text: Natural language processing (NLP) is one of the fastest growing field in computational linguistics and artificial intelligence, e.g. ACL has experienced a 140% growth from 2017 (1419 submissions) to 2020 (3429 submissions). Plus, there are more than 4000 pre-prints published at ArXiv in 2019. As a result, it has become increasingly stressful for researchers to keep up with the evolution of new methods. Today, the common way for researchers to find relevant papers is via searching keywords in Google Scholar 2 or Semantic Scholar 3 . Although these search engines are great at curating all the papers, they are limited in the following ways: (1) they are based on classic information retrieval methods, and do not handle natural language queries well, e.g. what effects can we get from label smoothing? (2) they are designed to find relevant documents (title and abstract) instead of direct answers to users' questions. Often researchers are looking for answers on specific research questions, e.g, how to prevent posterior collapse in VAE? or how much is it to label sentences via crowdsourcing? With current search engine, it requires one to read several papers to find these answer. Therefore, it is necessary to create better tools for researchers to find answers from the scientific publications in a more efficient manner.

text: We conduct side-by-side human evaluations to compare the outputs of our models. To evaluate image captioning model B against baseline model A, we generate captions for these images using both systems and task human annotators with their comparison. As depicted in Figure 4, raters are presented with an image alongside the two captions—randomly assigned to the left or right positions—and are asked to assess them using a side-by-side rating scale. Additionally, they are required to provide an absolute rating for each individual caption. These absolute scores serve as a cross-check for the comparative assessment. In our experiments, each image and its associated captions are evaluated by three separate raters.
source_text: We perform side-by-side human evaluation for comparing model outputs. To compare two image captioning models A (baseline) vs B, we generate captions for these images with each model and ask human raters to compare them. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the raters are shown the image with the two captions randomly placed to the left vs. right, and are asked to compare the captions on a side-by-side rating scale. In addition, they are asked to also provide an absolute rating for each caption. The absolute rating provides a cross-check on the comparison. Each image and associated captions are rated by three raters in our experiments.

text: Parallel sentences within the MSD dataset exhibit superior quality compared to SimpWiki, as our gold references demonstrate greater fluency (averaging 4.29 versus 7.65 in perplexity) and higher discriminability (averaging 91% versus 60% in style accuracy). The transfer process for L2E proves more challenging than for E2L, except regarding content similarity: metrics show 39.55% versus 42.50% in average accuracy, 11.50 versus 10.33 in average perplexity, and 2.80 versus 2.63 in average human ratings. This difficulty arises because elevating expertise levels necessitates more extensive context and knowledge, making it more arduous than simplification. TS models achieve performance levels comparable to ST models. Furthermore, the supervised OpenNMT+PT model outperforms the unsupervised UNTS in fluency and content similarity by leveraging additional supervision signals, whereas UNTS attains higher accuracy by utilizing a larger volume of non-parallel training data. Style accuracy and content similarity exhibit an inverse relationship, which complicates the development of a comprehensive evaluation metric capable of balancing these two conflicting objectives. In terms of content similarity, although both self-BLEU and ref-BLEU correlate strongly with human assessments (Pearson coefficients exceeding 0.98 with p-values < 0.0001), the elevated scores of ControlledGen do not indicate superior performance, as the model implements minimal stylistic modifications. Conversely, DeleteAndRetrieve demonstrates a robust capacity for style control (averaging 70% accuracy on MSD) but struggles to maintain content. Style Transformer displays more consistent performance.
source_text: (1) Parallel sentences in MSD have higher quality than SimpWiki, because our gold references are more fluent (4.29 vs. 7.65 in perplexity on average) and more discriminable (91% vs. 60% on average style accuracy). (2) The transfer for L2E is more difficult (except in content similarity) than that for E2L: 39.55% vs. 42.50% in Acc on average, 11.50 vs. 10.33 in PPL on average and 2.80 vs. 2.63 in human ratings on average. This is because the increase in expertise levels requires more contexts and knowledge, and is harder than simplification. (3) TS models perform similarly with ST models. Besides, supervised model OpenNMT+PT outperforms the unsupervised UNTS in fluency and content similarity due to the additional supervision signals. On the other hand, UNTS achieves higher Acc since it utilizes more non-parallel training data. (4) The style accuracy is the reverse to content sim-ilarity, making it more challenging to propose a comprehensive evaluation metric that can balance the two opposite directions. In terms of content similarity, even if both self-BLEU and ref-BLEU show a strong correlation with human ratings (over 0.98 Pearson coefficient with p-value< 0.0001), the higher scores of ControlledGen cannot demonstrate its superior performance, as it actually makes little modifications to styles. Instead, DeleteAn-dRetrieve, presents a strong ability to control styles (70% on average in Acc on MSD), but hardly preserves the contents. Style Transformer performs more stably.

text: In this study, we utilize demographically representative author samples from five languages (Dutch, English, French, German, and Italian) and translate them using three commercial machine translation systems: Google, Bing, and DeepL. We compare the ground-truth demographics with those predicted from each translation, alongside a control predictor trained on the original source languages. Regardless of the semantic quality of the translations, our findings demonstrate (a) significant discrepancies in perceived demographics and (b) a systematic tendency for translations to portray authors as older and more predominantly male than their actual profiles suggest.
source_text: In this paper, we use demographicallyrepresentative author samples from five languages (Dutch, English, French, German, Italian) , and translate them with three commercially available machine translation systems (Google, Bing, and DeepL). We compare the true demographics with the predicted demographics of each translation (as well as a control predictor trained on the same language). Without making any judgment on the translation of the content, we find a) that there are substantial discrepancies in the perceived demographics, and b) that translations tend to make the writers appear older and considerably more male than they are.

text: The primary objective of this paper is to predict the morphological word frequency (MWF) of derivatives—specifically, the probability that a word will emerge as an extension of a lexical cluster—by directly utilizing associative information. Given that connections within the mental lexicon encompass various semantic and formal similarities, many of which are not morphological (Tamariz, 2008), we aim to construct a distilled model of the mental lexicon restricted to derivational information. This is achieved through a derivational projection of the mental lexicon, a network architecture we designate as the Derivational Graph (DG).
source_text: The main goal of this paper is to predict the MWF of morphological derivatives (i.e., how likely is a word to be formed as an extension of a lexical cluster) by directly leveraging associative information. Since links in the mental lexicon reflect semantic and formal similarities of various sorts, many of which are not morphological (Tamariz, 2008) , we want to create a distilled model of the mental lexicon that only contains derivational information. One way to achieve this is by means of a derivational projection of the mental lexicon, a network that we call the Derivational Graph (DG).

text: Figure 3: An illustration of the OT loss, which is defined via OT distance to align key words between the table and the generated sentence. Left: the generated sentence contains extraneous information not found in the table (highlighted in orange) and lacks certain details present in the table (highlighted in red). This constitutes unfaithful generation, resulting in a high OT loss. Right: the generated sentence encompasses all information from the table and does not include any external content. This represents faithful generation, yielding a low OT cost. This example is adapted and modified from (Dhingra et al., 2019).
source_text: Figure 3: Illustration of the OT loss, which is defined with OT distance to only match key words in both the table and the generated sentence. Left: the generated sentence not only contains extra information not presented in the table (shown as orange), but also lacks some information presented in the table (shown as red). This is unfaithful generation. The OT lost is thus high. Right: all information in the table is covered in the generated sentence, and the generated sentence does not contain extra information not presented in the table. This is faithful generation. The OT cost is thus low. This example is borrowed and modified from (Dhingra et al., 2019) .

text: Question Generation (QG) systems are fundamental to question answering (QA), dialogue systems, and automated tutoring frameworks, as they augment QA training datasets, enable chatbots to initiate interactions with engaging queries, and automate the creation of assessment items. Current research in QG has predominantly focused on the production of factoid questions centered on a single piece of information within a sentence (Duan et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019), as depicted in Figure 1 a). However, the dimensions of comprehension and reasoning in questioning have received less attention, resulting in the generation of shallow questions that do not adequately reflect the sophisticated nature of human creative processes.
source_text: Question Generation (QG) systems play a vital role in question answering (QA), dialogue system, and automated tutoring applications -by enriching the training QA corpora, helping chatbots start conversations with intriguing questions, and automatically generating assessment questions, respectively. Existing QG research has typically focused on generating factoid questions relevant to one fact obtainable from a single sentence (Duan et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019) , as exemplified in Figure 1 a) . However, less explored has been the comprehension and reasoning aspects of questioning, resulting in questions that are shallow and not reflective of the true creative human process.

text: This paper investigates the performance ceiling of machine translation (MT) systems when restricted to a bilingual lexicon and extensive monolingual corpora. This setup emulates the human process of acquiring translation proficiency through dictionary consultation without access to parallel sentence pairs. We address this task by introducing anchored training, a method that integrates the bilingual dictionary into the MT framework to align the latent representations of both languages, thereby facilitating more effective translation. Empirical evaluations across both related and typologically distant language pairs demonstrate that our proposed approach successfully narrows the divergence between source and target language spaces. Consequently, our method achieves substantial improvements in translation quality compared to MT baselines that exclude dictionary information and those that utilize the lexicon solely for supervised cross-lingual word embedding transformations.
source_text: In this paper, we explore how much potential an MT system can achieve when only using a bilingual dictionary and large-scale monolingual corpora. This task simulates people acquiring translation ability via looking up the dictionary and depending on no parallel sentence examples. We propose to tackle the task by injecting the bilingual dictionary into MT via anchored training that drives both language spaces closer so that the translation becomes easier. Experiments show that, on both close language pairs and distant language pairs, our proposed approach effectively reduces the gap between the source language space and the target language space, leading to significant improvement of translation quality over the MT approaches that do not use the dictionary and the approaches that use the dictionary to supervise the cross-lingual word embedding transformation.

text: Gold Lexicons and Data Splits. We employ the English emotion lexicon from Warriner et al. (2013) as the initial component of our Source dataset. This widely used resource consists of approximately 14,000 entries in VAD format, collected via crowdsourcing. As manual BE5 ratings are available for only a subset of this lexicon (Stevenson et al., 2007), we incorporate BE5 ratings from Buechel and Hahn (2018a). Their work utilized emotion representation mapping (see Section 2) to convert the existing VAD ratings, demonstrating that this approach is nearly as reliable as human annotation.
source_text: Gold Lexicons and Data Splits. We use the English emotion lexicon from Warriner et al. (2013) as first part of our Source dataset. This popular resource comprises about 14k entries in VAD format collected via crowdsourcing. Since manually gathered BE5 ratings are available only for a subset of this lexicon (Stevenson et al., 2007) , we add BE5 ratings from Buechel and Hahn (2018a) who used emotion representation mapping (see Section 2) to convert the existing VAD ratings, showing that this is about as reliable as human annotation.

text: The effective representation of arguments is a fundamental aspect of our task. Most existing research maps arguments into dense, continuous vector spaces using various neural architectures, ranging from basic CNNs and RNNs to more complex neural networks (Zhang et al., 2015; Qin et al., 2016b; Rutherford et al., 2016). Certain approaches have adopted distinct attention or memory mechanisms to emphasize key elements within discourse arguments (Mnih et al., 2014); for instance, hierarchical attention is used to capture focus across different granularities, while semantic memory is utilized to store task-related knowledge in a distributed manner. Nevertheless, these models typically process the two arguments as independent entities, thereby overlooking the critical interaction information between them.
source_text: Proper argument representation is a core factor of our task. Most previous researches encode arguments as dense and continuous representation based on various neural networks, from basic neural networks (such as CNN, RNN) to complex neural networks (Zhang et al., 2015; Qin et al., 2016b; Rutherford et al., 2016) . Some studies adopt different attention or memory mechanisms to catch the emphasis on discourse arguments (Mnih et al., 2014; . exploit the hierarchical attention to capture the focus of different granularities. build upon a semantic memory to store knowledge in the distributed fashion for the task. However, these models have only considered the two arguments independently without the interaction information.

text: During training, we organized translation pairs into mini-batches based on comparable sequence lengths. Table 1 details the batch sizes employed for our primary experiments, where batch size is defined as the total number of source tokens. In the analyses conducted in Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 5.4, datasets with fewer than 500k sentence pairs utilized a vocabulary and batch size of 4k, whereas larger datasets used 32k. While the models described in the main text were trained on lowercased data, we provide supplementary experiments involving the original casing and case-sensitive BLEU metrics in the appendix.
source_text: In training, translation pairs were batched together by approximate sequence length. For the main experiments, the values of batch size we used are given in Table 1 (batch size is the number of source tokens). In the experiments in Sections 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4, for datasets not larger than 500k sentence pairs we use vocabulary size and batch size of 4k, and 32k for the rest. 4 In the main text, we train all models on lowercased data. In the appendix, we provide additional experiments with the original case and casesensitive BLEU.

text: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews recent related work. In Section 3, we present, compare, and analyze the corpora used to train our contextualized embeddings, as well as the treebanks employed for our POS tagging and parsing models. Section 4 provides a detailed description of the model used for our contextualized word representations, alongside the parser and tagger selected to evaluate the impact of training corpora on embedding performance in downstream tasks. Finally, Section 5 offers an analysis of our results, and Section 6 presents our conclusions.
source_text: The structure of the paper is as follows. In Section 2 we describe the recent related work. In Section 3 we present, compare and analyze the corpora used to train our contextualized embeddings, and the treebanks used to train our POS tagging and parsing models. In Section 4 we examine and describe in detail the model used for our contextualized word representations, as well as the parser and the tagger we chose to evaluate the impact of corpora in the embeddings' performance in downstream tasks. Finally we provide an analysis of our results in Section 5 and in Section 6 we present our conclusions.

text: The BiRNN model employs an encoder-decoder architecture, receiving both the source sentence and its translation as inputs, which are encoded independently by two bidirectional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). The two resulting sentence representations are integrated via an attention mechanism that computes a weighted sum of their constituent word vectors. For sentence-level predictions, this joint weighted representation is passed through a dense layer with sigmoid activation to produce quality estimates. To perform document-level predictions, a second attention mechanism generates a final document representation by calculating the weighted sum of the sentence-level representations for all sentences within the text. This document-level representation is subsequently processed by a dense layer with sigmoid activation to generate the final quality estimates.
source_text: The BiRNN model uses an encoder-decoder architecture: it takes on its input both the source sentence and its translation which are encoded separately by two independent bi-directional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). The two resulting sentence representations are then concatenated as a weighted sum of their word vectors, generated by an attention mechanism. For sentence-level predictions, the weighted representation of the two input sentences is passed through a dense layer with sigmoid activation to generate the quality estimates. For document-level predictions, the final representation of a document is generated by a second attention mechanism, as the weighted sum of the weighted sentence-level representations of all the sentences within the document. The resulting document-level representation is then passed through a dense layer with sigmoid activation to generate the quality estimates.

text: Consequently, our evaluation framework is designed to satisfy two primary criteria: first, assessment must exclude any translational counterparts of Source entries utilized during the training phase (e.g., *Sonnenschein* and *nuklear* in Figure 1); second, the evaluation set must comprise a sufficient number of instances to ensure statistical reliability. Our generation methodology utilizes a sophisticated cross-lingual data partitioning strategy to reconcile these requirements. Specifically, for our silver evaluation, we determine the intersection of TargetMT-test and TargetPred-test and calculate the correlation for each emotion variable within this subset. We employ Pearson’s $r$ as the standard correlation metric throughout this work. By establishing the Source-test set at the beginning of our workflow, we ensure a substantial overlap between these sets, thereby fulfilling our evaluation objectives.
source_text: Thus, our evaluation methodology needs to fulfill the following two requirements: (1) evaluation must not be performed on translational equivalents of the Source entries to which the model already had access during training (e.g., Sonnenschein and nuklear in our example from Figure 1) ; but, on the other hand, (2) a reasonable number of instances must be available for evaluation (ideally, as many as possible to increase reliability). The intricate cross-lingual train-dev-test set assignment of our generation methodology is in place so that we meet these two requirements. In particular, for our silver evaluation, we intersect TargetMT-test with TargetPred-test and compute the correlation of these two sets individually for each emotion variable. Pearson's r will be used as correlation measure throughout this paper. Establishing a test set at the very start of our workflow, Source-test, assures that there is a relatively large overlap between the two sets and, by extension, that our requirements for the evaluation are met.

text: Additionally, we perform a transferability analysis where models trained on the MuTual dataset are evaluated directly on MuTual plus without further fine-tuning. This setup examines the models' capacity to process safe responses when such instances are absent from the training distribution. As illustrated in Table 4, the performance of RoBERTa-MC and RoBERTa declines by 24.1% and 6.8%, respectively, under this transfer condition, highlighting the importance of including safe responses in the training data. Furthermore, the individual scoring RoBERTa variant exhibits superior performance compared to RoBERTa-MC, suggesting that the individual scoring approach offers greater robustness in scenarios where safe responses are not encountered during training.
source_text: Furthermore, we conduct a transfer experiment, in which models are trained on MuTual but tested on MuTual plus without fine-tuning. The experiment investigates whether the model handles safe responses well if they have never seen them in training corpus. As shown in Table 4 , RoBERTa-MC and RoBERTa drops 24.1% and 6.8%, respectively, in the transfer setting, demonstrating the benefits of seeing safe responses during the training process. Moreover, the individual scoring RoBERTa outperforms RoBERTa-MC, showing that the individual scoring method is more robust, when the safe response is not fed during training.

text: The JApanese MUlti-Length Headline Corpus (JA-MUL) 6 is a dataset specifically developed for the evaluation of summarization techniques. JA-MUL comprises 1,524 full-text Japanese articles and their corresponding newspaper print headlines. While this corpus is available free of charge, it does not provide sufficient data to train an encoder-decoder model independently. Hitomi et al. (2019) also introduced the Japanese News Corpus (JNC), an extensive dataset containing 1,831,812 pairs of newspaper articles and headlines. The JNC, however, includes only the initial three sentences of each article 7.
source_text: JApanese MUlti-Length Headline Corpus (JA-MUL) 6 is a dataset specially designed for evaluating summarization methods. JAMUL consists of 1,524 Japanese full-text articles and their print headlines (used for newspapers). Although JAMUL is distributed for free of charge, JAMUL alone is insufficient for training an encoder-decoder model. Hitomi et al. (2019) also released Japanese News Corpus (JNC), which is a large-scale dataset consisting of 1,831,812 pairs of newspaper articles and their print headlines. JNC includes only the first three sentences of each article 7 .

text: To facilitate information understanding, organization, and recommendation, our system synthesizes data concerning individuals extracted from various input streams. Beyond standard external knowledge base linking, the architecture performs coreference resolution for person entities and utilizes text-visual grounding to associate images with their surrounding textual context. Consequently, addressing privacy implications is of paramount importance (Tucker, 2019). While this demonstration relies on public online datasets distributed by the LDC 21, adapting the system to alternative sources necessitates a careful evaluation of privacy risks relative to the specific application and its underlying data. More broadly, we advocate for comprehensive end-to-end algorithmic auditing prior to any practical deployment of the software (Raji et al., 2020).
source_text: To understand, organize, and recommend information, our system aggregates information about people as reported in its input sources. For example, in addition to external KB linking, the system performs coreference on named people and uses text-visual grounding to link images to the surrounding context. Privacy concerns thus merit at-tention (Tucker, 2019) . The demo relies on publicly available, online sources released by the LDC 21 . When applying our system to other sources, care should be paid to privacy with respect to the intended application and the data that it uses. More generally, end-to-end algorithmic auditing should be conducted before the deployment of our software (Raji et al., 2020) .

text: Computing emission scores, which in this context refer to word-label similarities, remains a significant challenge. Widely used few-shot models, such as Prototypical Networks (Snell et al., 2017), typically represent labels by averaging the embeddings of their support examples; however, these representations often cluster closely in the embedding space, frequently resulting in misclassification. To mitigate this issue, Yoon et al. (2019) introduced TapNet, which learns to project embeddings into a space where words belonging to different labels are well-separated. We adapt this concept for slot tagging and propose to enhance label representations by leveraging the semantics of label names. We argue that label names are often semantically linked to slot words, a relationship that can significantly aid word-label similarity modeling. For example, as illustrated in Figure 1, the word "rain" and the label "weather" are highly correlated. To exploit label name semantics and ensure distinct label representations, we propose Label-enhanced TapNet (L-TapNet), which constructs a projection space using label semantics where representations are well-separated and aligned with both label name and slot word embeddings. Similarities are subsequently calculated within this projected space. Furthermore, we introduce a pair-wise embedding mechanism to represent words within domain-specific contexts.
source_text: It is also challenging to compute the emission scores (word-label similarity in our case). Popular few-shot models, such as Prototypical Network (Snell et al., 2017) , average the embeddings of each label's support examples as label representations, which often distribute closely in the embedding space and thus cause misclassification. To remedy this, Yoon et al. 2019propose TapNet that learns to project embedding to a space where words of different labels are well-separated. We introduce this idea to slot tagging and further propose to improve label representation by leveraging the semantics of label names. We argue that label names are often semantically related to slot words and can help word-label similarity modeling. For example in Figure 1 , word rain and label name weather are highly related. To use label name semantic and achieve good-separating in label representation, we propose Label-enhanced TapNet (L-TapNet) that constructs an embedding projection space using label name semantics, where label representations are well-separated and aligned with embeddings of both label name and slot words. Then we calculate similarities in the projected embedding space. Also, we introduce a pair-wise embedding mechanism to representation words with domain-specific context.

text: The integration of HCN with machine teaching proves highly effective for three primary reasons. First, dialogue authors are typically subject matter experts equipped to make authoritative decisions regarding the specific actions a dialogue manager (DM) should take in given contexts. If the DM were instead required to autonomously derive an action policy from logs, an extensive data corpus would be necessary. Second, the HCN framework allows authors to directly incorporate domain-specific knowledge through action templates—defining bot activities or responses—and action masks, which specify when certain responses are prohibited, without the need for learning. Third, intelligent filtering can be applied to isolate the most significant failed dialogues for review by teachers.
source_text: There are three main reasons the HCN + machine teaching combination is so effective. First, dialog authors are generally subject matter experts who can make well-informed decisions about which actions the DM should perform in individual situations. If the DM has to automatically learn an action policy from logs, a large corpus of data is required. Second, the HCN allows dialog authors to explicitly encode domain-specific knowledge as action templates (bot activities or responses) and action masks (when a given response should be disallowed) without learning. Third, we can use intelligent filtering to select the most impactful failed dialogs for teachers to review.

text: Intrinsic hallucinations emerge when models synthesize content derived from the input document but distort the underlying facts. For instance, as illustrated in Figure 1, the descriptions "Former London mayoral candidate" in the TCONVS2S abstract and "Former London mayor" in the TRANS2S abstract represent intrinsic hallucinations; while these phrases utilize terms present in the source text, they misinterpret the original information and are thus unfaithful to the document. The source material does not verify whether "Zac Goldsmith" was a "Former London mayoral candidate" or if "Sadiq Khan" served as a "Former London mayor." Such errors likely stem from inadequate input representations that hinder the document-level inference necessary for effective abstraction. Conversely, extrinsic hallucinations occur when generated outputs include information entirely absent from the source. Examples from Figure 1 include the mention of "Nigel" in the PTGEN abstract and "2016" in both the GOLD and GPT-TUNED summaries, as these entities are never introduced in the text. Models equipped with poorly informed decoders that ignore the divergence between source and target distributions (Wiseman et al., 2017; Dhingra et al., 2019) tend to function as unconstrained language models, making them highly susceptible to extrinsic hallucinations.
source_text: Intrinsic hallucinations are consequences of synthesizing content using the information present in the input document. For example, in Figure 1, "Former London mayoral candidate" in the TCONVS2S abstract and "Former London mayor" in the TRANS2S abstract are hallucinations of intrinsic nature; both use terms or concepts from the document but misrepresent information from the document, making them unfaithful to the document. The article does not confirm if "Zac Goldsmith" was a "Former London mayoral candidate" or if "Sadiq Khan" was a "Former London mayor". One may suspect that a model with poor input document representation will fail to do document level inference, often required for abstraction, and will be vulnerable to such errors. Extrinsic hallucinations are model generations that ignore the source material altogether. For example, in Figure 1 , "Nigel" in the PTGEN abstract and "2016" in both GOLD and GPT-TUNED are extrinsic hallucinations; these terms are not introduced in the document. A model with a poorlyinformed decoder and that is agnostic to the divergence issue between the source and target texts (Wiseman et al., 2017; Dhingra et al., 2019) , will function more as an open-ended language model and will be prone to extrinsic hallucinations.

text: We evaluate CLIReval by conducting a case study on the WMT2019 metrics shared task (Ma et al., 2019), which assesses the Pearson correlation ($r$) between automated MT metrics and human gold standards. Our results indicate that CLIReval consistently achieves $r \geq 0.9$, performing competitively with or exceeding established metrics like BLEU across various language pairs. Notably, these benchmarks are reached without the use of external data or domain-specific parameter optimization. These encouraging findings underscore the potential of CLIR as an effective proxy for machine translation evaluation, and we envision that CLIReval will support and encourage further exploration in this research area.
source_text: As a case study, we test CLIReval on the metrics shared task of WMT2019 (Ma et al., 2019) , which measures the Pearson correlations (r) between automatically generated MT metrics and human judgments. Results show that CLIReval consistently performs at the level of r ≥ 0.9 and is on par or even outperforms popular metrics such as BLEU on multiple language directions. Further, this is achieved without using external data or doing domain-based parameter tuning. These promising results highlight the potential of CLIR as a proxy task for MT evaluation, and we hope CLIReval can facilitate future research in this area.

text: This paper introduces Xiaomingbot, a multilingual and multimodal architecture for automated news reporting. The system's end-to-end workflow comprises several discrete stages. First, news articles are synthesized using a template-based table-to-text technique, which are then condensed via an extractive summarization module. These summaries are subsequently translated into multiple target languages. The pipeline concludes with the generation of a video featuring an animated avatar delivering the report through speech synthesis. By employing a voice cloning model that facilitates few-shot learning from limited Chinese audio samples, Xiaomingbot ensures consistent prosody and vocal identity across linguistic boundaries. To date, Xiaomingbot has been successfully deployed and is currently operational for end-users.
source_text: In this paper, we present Xiaomingbot, a multilingual and multi-modal system for news reporting. The entire process of Xiaomingbot's news reporting can be condensed as follows. First, it learns how to write news articles based on a template based table2text technology, and summarize the news through an extraction based method. Next, its system translates the summarization into multiple languages. Finally, the system produces the video of an animated avatar reading the news with synthesized voice. Owing to the voice cloning model that can learn from a few Chinese audio samples, Xiaomingbot can maintain consistency in intonation and voice projection across different languages. So far, Xiaomingbot has been deployed online and is serving users.

text: While current state-of-the-art abstractive summarization models yield ROUGE-1 scores below 40 on the Gigaword benchmark, the resulting headlines exhibit a high degree of linguistic fluency. This quality is likely a consequence of the encoder-decoder architecture internalizing a robust language model from extensive supervised training data. Nevertheless, several studies have observed that these generated headlines frequently diverge from the source document's underlying content (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019). These works specifically highlight instances where abstractive models introduce factual inconsistencies, such as errors involving subject-predicate-object tuples.
source_text: Although the current state-of-the-art method for abstractive summarization could only achieve a ROUGE-1 score of less than 40 on the Gigaword dataset, generated headlines actually look very fluent. This is probably because the encoder-decoder model acquired a strong language model from the vast amount of supervision data. However, some studies reported that the generated headlines often deviate from the content of the original document (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019) . They addressed the problem where an abstractive model made mistakes in facts (e.g., tuples of subjects, predicates, and objects).

text: Evaluation Metrics. We adopt three standard automatic metrics frequently utilized in previous research. BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) are derived using SacreBLEU (Ma et al., 2019), while chrF++ (Popović, 2017) is calculated through both SacreBLEU and the evaluation scripts released by the baseline system authors. We also compute METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) employing the default English configurations of the CMU implementation. In addition to these traditional benchmarks, we perform human evaluation and utilize BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) to assess semantic similarity. These methods are notably less reliant on the surface-level tokens of the gold-standard references. This distinction is vital for the AMR-to-text task, as a single AMR graph may correspond to multiple valid sentential realizations with identical meanings. Traditional metrics often overemphasize surface form overlap and thus struggle to account for a system's ability to produce semantically consistent but lexically diverse sentences.
source_text: Metrics. We considered the three automatic evaluation metrics commonly used in previous works. We compute BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) using SacreBLEU (Ma et al., 2019) . We compute chrF++ (Popović, 2017) using both SacreBLEU and the scripts used by authors of the baseline systems. We compute METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) with the default values for English of the CMU implementation. 2 In addition to the standard automatic metrics, we also carry out human evaluation experiments and use the semantic similarity metric BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) . Both metrics arguably have less dependency on the surface symbols of the reference text used for evaluation. This is particularly relevant for the AMR-to-text task, since one single AMR graph corresponds to multiple sentences with the same semantic meaning. Conventional metrics for AMR-to-text are are strongly influenced by surface symbols and thus do not capture well the ability of the system to produce a diverse sentences with same underlying semantics.

text: Encoder. The encoder $g_\theta$ incorporates the adjacency matrix $B$ of the directed graph (DG) $B$ as a primary input. This allows us to represent the stem and affix group forms, $f_s$ and $f_a$, through the associative connections they establish within the mental lexicon. Because the DG does not account for semantic relationships between nodes in $S$ and $A$, we reintroduce semantic content via auxiliary feature vectors $x_s, x_a \in \mathbb{R}^n$ for the stem and affix group embeddings $m_s$ and $m_a$, which are trained independently on the SR corpora. Consequently, the input to $g_\theta$ is structured to provide synergistic information: the associative framework provided by $B$ and the semantic representations captured in $x_s$ and $x_a$.
source_text: Encoder. The encoder g θ takes as one of its inputs the adjacency matrix B of the DG B. This means we model f s and f a , the stem and affix group forms, by means of the associative relationships they create in the mental lexicon. Since a DG has no information about semantic relationships between nodes within S and A, we reintroduce meaning as additional feature vectors x s , x a ∈ R n for m s and m a , stem and affix group embeddings that are trained separately on the SR texts. The input to g θ is thus designed to provide complementary information: associative information (B) and semantic information (x s and x a ).

text: A distinctive feature of MT-DNN is its out-of-the-box support for adversarial training, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation. This framework allows users to train related tasks jointly, facilitating mutual performance gains through cross-task synergy. Users can also implement adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) to enhance both model robustness and generalizability. To overcome the practical challenges of large-scale production deployment, MT-DNN enables the compression of original models into significantly smaller versions, even when utilizing different architectures—such as distilling BERT or other Transformer-based encoders into LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997). This distillation process can itself leverage multi-task learning and adversarial training. Additionally, users can perform pretraining from scratch using the masked language modeling objective. During fine-tuning, this objective can be integrated as an auxiliary task on the training text, a method shown to improve performance. Finally, MT-DNN offers a comprehensive suite of state-of-the-art pre-trained NLU models, along with step-by-step tutorials for their application in general and biomedical domains.
source_text: A key distinct feature for MT-DNN is that it provides out-of-box adversarial training, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation. Users can train a set of related tasks jointly to amplify each other. They can also invoke adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) , which helps improve model robustness and generalizability. For production deployment where large model size becomes a practical obstacle, users can use MT-DNN to compress the original models into substantially smaller ones, even using a completely different architecture (e.g., compressed BERT or other Transformer-based text encoders into LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) ). The distillation step can similarly leverage multitask learning and adversarial training. Users can also conduct pretraining from scratch using the masked language model objective in MT-DNN. Moreover, in the fine-tuning step, users can incorporate this as an auxiliary task on the training text, which has been shown to improve performance. MT-DNN provides a comprehensive list of stateof-the-art pre-trained NLU models, together with step-by-step tutorials for using such models in general and biomedical applications.

text: In the context of translating into a uniform target language like English, BLEU scores are comparable and serve as a reliable proxy for translation difficulty. This allows for the conclusion that Lithuanian (lt) is the most challenging source language, while Spanish (es) is the least difficult. Given that BLEU scores align well with human judgment, it is desirable for XMI to exhibit a strong correlation with BLEU. This pattern is indeed visible in the blue points on the left side of Figure 2, confirming that XMI is an effective metric for evaluating translation difficulty while remaining independent of the target language generation component.
source_text: Translating into English When translating into the same target language (in this case, English), BLEU scores are, in fact, comparable, and can be used as a proxy for difficulty. We can then conclude, for instance, that Lithuanian (lt) is the hardest language to translate from, while Spanish (es) is the easiest. In this scenario, given the good correlation of BLEU scores with human evaluations, it is desirable that XMI correlates well with BLEU. This behavior is indeed apparent in the blue points in the left part of Figure 2 , confirming the efficacy of XMI in evaluating the difficulty of translation while still being independent of the target language generation component.

text: Position-Aware Graphs. Zheng and Lapata (2019) introduced PacSum, a graph-based approach for identifying salient sentences across multiple documents. Within this framework, a sentence's importance is weighted such that higher selection probability is assigned to nodes demonstrating greater average similarity with subsequent sentences compared to preceding ones. This mechanism allows PacSum to emphasize sentences that are both "semantically central" and located in early document positions. We extend this methodology by employing SBERT for similarity computations—denoted as SPS—and investigate both the individual and global graph variants of this approach.
source_text: Position-Aware Graphs. PacSum is a recently proposed graph-based method to select salient sentences from multiple documents (Zheng and Lapata, 2019) . In PacSum, a sentence is more likely to be selected if it has higher average similarity with its succeeding sentences and lower average similarity with its preceding sentences. This strategy allows PacSum to prioritize the selection of earlyposition and "semantically central" sentences. We further extend PacSum by using SBERT to measure sentences similarity (the resulting method is denoted as SPS) and consider both the individualand global-graph versions of SPS.

text: Driven by neural-based progress in parsing techniques, NLP researchers and practitioners can now generate accurate, large-scale syntactically-annotated corpora. However, the practical application and empirical advantages of these dependency structures remain limited. While basic syntactic dependencies capture functional links between words, they fail to represent many functional and semantic associations that occur between the content words of a sentence. Furthermore, the reliance on purely syntactic relations results in structural variation, which complicates efforts to effectively extract coherent semantic information from the generated structures.
source_text: Owing to neural-based advances in parsing technology, NLP researchers and practitioners can now accurately produce syntactically-annotated corpora at scale. However, the use and empirical benefits of the dependency structures themselves remain limited. Basic syntactic dependencies encode the functional connections between words but lack many functional and semantic relations that exist between the content words in the sentence. Moreover, the use of strictly-syntactic relations results in structural diversity, undermining the efforts to effectively extract coherent semantic information from the resulting structures.

text: When dealing with linguistically related source and target languages, such as in the FR+ES→EN model, we find a high positive correlation with bilingual counterparts, suggesting that alignment learning can proceed in parallel. Conversely, the inclusion of a distant source language (ZH) during training results in a reduction in correlation for FR and ES, with a more substantial decline observed for ZH. This outcome corresponds with the BLEU results discussed in Section 5.1, indicating that multilingual training on distant input languages is inherently more difficult than on similar ones. The ConvTransformer exhibits greater resilience to the addition of a distant language than the standard Transformer (p < 0.005 for FR and ES inputs, as determined by a one-way ANOVA). These findings further imply that more advanced attention architectures may be required for the effective multilingual training of models involving several distant source languages.
source_text: For similar source and target languages (e.g., the FR+ES→EN model), we observe a strong pos- itive correlation to the bilingual models, indicating that alignments can be simultaneously learned. When introducing a distant source language (ZH) in the training, we observe a drop in correlation, for FR and ES, and an even larger drop for ZH. This result is in line with our BLEU results from Section 5.1, suggesting that multilingual training on distant input languages is more challenging than multilingual training on similar input languages. The convtransformer is more robust to the introduction of a distant language than the transformer (p < 0.005 for FR and ES inputs, according to a one-way ANOVA test). Our results also suggest that more sophisticated attention architectures might need to be developed when training multilingual models on several distant input languages.

text: To facilitate cross-domain transfer learning, we propose a domain-agnostic abstract model of conversational structures. This research specifically targets transactional dialogues, noting that alternative interaction types, such as opinion exchange, would require distinct modeling approaches. We introduce an algorithm that utilizes a target domain ontology and a small set of representative phrases to synthesize training data based on this abstract framework. The generated dialogues consist of discrete conversational turns, each defined by an initial state, an agent utterance, a user response, and a subsequent terminal state. These states serve to encapsulate the semantic context of the interaction at their respective points. A representative example of a dialogue synthesized via our proposed method is illustrated in Figure 1.
source_text: To facilitate transfer learning, we create an abstract model of dialogues that is independent of the domain of the conversation. In this paper we will focus on dialogues for transactions; other kinds of dialogues such as opinion sharing will have different models. We have developed an algorithm that accepts an ontology of a domain and a few phrases commonly used in that domain. The algorithm synthesizes dialogue training data based on an abstract dialogue model. The dialogue synthesized consists of turns of conversation, each of which has a start state, an agent utterance, a user utterance, and an end state. The start and end states summarize the semantics of the conversation at those points. An example of a dialogue that can be synthesized by our model is shown in Fig. 1 .

text: Chomsky (1976) conceptualizes metalinguistics as the formal knowledge of language structures and properties manifested across the domains of phonology, morphology, syntax, and semantics. Although a proficient English speaker maintains the latent linguistic ability to generate the term "undo" when prompted for the opposite of "do," truly reflecting upon the word’s morphological architecture requires a degree of metalinguistic awareness. This higher-order cognitive capacity allows the speaker to categorize "un-" as a prefix dedicated to negation and to observe its functional parallels with other morphemes such as "dis-" and "de-." Moreover, such an individual would recognize that "un-" is subject to distinct distributional constraints, as it typically prepends to verbs and adjectives rather than nouns, rendering it non-interchangeable with its functional counterparts.
source_text: Meta-linguistics is defined by Chomsky (1976) as "the knowledge of the characteristics and structures of language" as realised on the level of phonology, morphology, syntax and semantics. Any English speaker would likely have the linguistic capacity to produce the word undo when asked "What is the opposite of do?" Only a speaker with some level of meta-linguistic awareness, however, would further be able to reflect on the structure of the word they have produced: to identify unas a unit that serves to negate words, to spot its similarity in function to other units like disand de-. He/she would also be aware that unis not interchangeable with disand de-, since it attaches to the front of verbs and adjectives but not to nouns.

text: To decouple the effects of model class and data scale from test suite difficulty, we represent an individual trained model's performance on each test suite as a delta relative to the average performance of all models on that suite. Unless otherwise noted, the remaining figures in this section plot these score deltas, aggregated by model class or corpus type. Figure 3 illustrates the influence of model architecture and data scale across the types evaluated in our experiments, with SG score deltas on the y-axis. The left panel shows the variation in SG scores by model class, revealing that architecture significantly impacts performance; for example, the error bars—representing bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals of the mean—for RNNG and LSTM do not overlap. The right panel displays SG score deltas by training dataset, indicating a much more marginal increase in mean performance as the volume of training data grows.
source_text: In order to decouple the effects of model class and data scale from test suite difficulty, we represent a particular trained model's performance on each test suite as a delta relative to the average performance of all models on this test suite. Unless noted otherwise, the remainder of the figures in this section plot a score delta, aggregating these deltas within model classes or corpus types. Figure 3 tracks the influence of model class and data scale across the model types tested in our experiments, with SG score deltas on the y-axis. The left-hand panel shows the difference in SG score by model class. We find that model class clearly influences SG score: for example, the error bars (bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals of the mean) for RNNG and LSTM do not overlap. The right-hand panel shows the difference in SG score delta by training dataset, and shows a much more minor increase in mean SG score as training data increases.

text: Natural language interfaces to databases (NLIDBs) (Popescu et al., 2003; Li and Jagadish, 2014) facilitate broader access to relational data for end users and have remained a central focus of research for several decades (Hemphill et al., 1990; Dahl et al., 1994; Zelle and Mooney, 1996; Popescu et al., 2003; Bertomeu et al., 2006; Zhong et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018, 2019a). Traditional NLIDB architectures are frequently modular, combining rule-based parsing with components for ambiguity detection and pragmatics modeling (Li and Jagadish, 2014; Setlur et al., 2016, 2019). For instance, as illustrated in the PHOTON workflow in Figure 1, the system utilizes a question corrector to identify untranslatable user queries and pinpoint spans necessitating clarification; once validated, the input is processed by a text-to-SQL model to generate a query, with the resulting data finally returned to the user. While these rule-based frameworks have proven effective in pilot studies and production environments, they remain constrained by limited coverage, scalability issues, and a lack of naturalness. Specifically, such systems lack robustness against the inherent diversity of human expression and present significant challenges when attempting to scale across different domains.
source_text: Natural language interfaces to databases (Popescu et al., 2003; Li and Jagadish, 2014) democratize end user access to relational data and have attracted significant research attention for decades (Hemphill et al., 1990; Dahl et al., 1994; Zelle and Mooney, 1996; Popescu et al., 2003; Bertomeu et al., 2006; Zhong et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018 Yu et al., , 2019a . Most existing NLIDBs adopt a modular architecture consisting of rule-based natural language parsing, ambiguity detection and pragmatics modeling (Li and Figure 1 : PHOTON workflow. The question corrector (upper block) detects the untranslatable questions from user input, scans the confusion span(s) that need clarification or correction. The accepted question is mapped into a SQL query through a text-to-SQL model, and finally the SQL execution results are returned to the user. Jagadish, 2014; Setlur et al., 2016 Setlur et al., , 2019 . While they have been shown effective in pilot study and production, rule-based approaches are limited in terms of coverage, scalability and naturalnessthey are not robust against the diversity of human language expressions and are difficult to scale across domains.

text: The initial dataset comprises 85,000 sentences randomly selected from a substantially larger, balanced, and representative corpus of contemporary Polish. Leveraging existing human annotations, we adapted this dataset for a named entity classification task. We first excluded all sentences containing entities of more than one type. Subsequently, we partitioned the sentences into training, development, and test sets such that each unique named entity appears in only one of the three splits; this entity-based partitioning was intended to increase the difficulty of the task. To improve class balance, we undersampled the *persName* category and merged the *date* and *time* classes. Finally, we sampled sentences devoid of named entities to represent the *noEntity* class.
source_text: The original dataset consists of 85k sentences, randomly selected from a much larger, balanced and representative corpus of contemporary Polish. We use existing human-annotations of named entities to convert the dataset into a named entity classification task. First, we filter out all sentences with entities of more than one type. Then, we randomly assigned sentences into training, development and test sets in such a way, that each named entity appears only in one of the three splits. We decided to split the sentences based on named entities to make the task more difficult. To increase class balance, we undersample the persName class and merge date and time classes. Finally, we sample sentences without any named entity to represent the noEntity class.

text: The efficacy of the basic bigram language model and greedy TSP decoding is fundamentally tied to the Tree-LSTM encoding. To highlight its significance, we ablate the tree encoding for each linearizer, restricting them to token-level feature representations. We evaluate four linearizers: the beam, sub, and full models used in the primary experiments, as well as a swap linearizer trained to sort randomized input sequences. The +tree condition denotes the default configuration, while -tree signifies the exclusion of tree encoding. Notably, in the -tree setting, beam and sub still leverage tree structure to partition trees into subtrees, whereas full and swap utilize no tree information whatsoever. The results, detailed in Table 5, show that the performance decline for sub is more substantial than for beam when the tree encoder is removed. This implies that sub is more dependent on the robust representations of the Tree-LSTM, as its scoring function—effectively a bigram language model—is significantly less expressive than the LSTM in beam in the absence of syntax. This finding offers a compelling analogy to first-order graph-based dependency parsers (Kiperwasser and Goldberg, 2016; Dozat and Manning, 2016), which also surpass transition-based models by employing a simpler scoring function that remains free from error propagation.
source_text: The success of the simple bigram language model and greedy TSP decoding relies heavily on the Tree-LSTM encoding. To demonstrate its importance, we remove the tree encoding for each linearizer, i.e., they only receive the token level features as the representation. We experiment with four linearizers: apart from beam, sub and full as in the main experiments, we also include the swap linearizer that is trained to sort random input sequences. The condition +tree is the default case, while in -tree we do not use the tree encoding. Note that in the latter case, beam and sub still use the tree information to split the tree into subtrees, while full and swap do not use the tree information in any way. The results are shown in Table 5 . Without the tree encoder, the performance drop in sub is larger than beam, which suggests that sub is more dependent on the good representation of the Tree-LSTM encoder, since its scoring function is essentially a bigram language model, which would be much less expressive than the LSTM in beam if syntax is absent. This result draws an interesting analogy to the fact that first-order graph-based dependency parsers (Kiperwasser and Goldberg, 2016; Dozat and Manning, 2016 ) also outperform the transition-based counterparts with a simpler scoring model but without error propagation.

text: Prior research on bridging anaphora resolution within the ISNotes and BASHI datasets typically utilizes gold or system mentions as antecedent candidates, with performance measured in terms of strict accuracy (Hou et al., 2013b; Hou, 2018a). To facilitate a direct comparison with these benchmarks, we map the top 20 span predictions from our BARQA system for each bridging anaphor $a$ to the corresponding gold or system mentions, selecting the candidate with the highest confidence score as the final predicted antecedent. Specifically, a predicted span $s$ is mapped to a mention $m$ if they share the same syntactic head and $s$ is a substring of $m$ (where $m$ is derived by stripping all postmodifiers). For example, the span "total potential claims" is mapped to the mention "the total potential claims from the disaster." Spans that cannot be mapped to any gold or system mentions are discarded. Furthermore, following Hou (2018a), if $a$ is a temporal expression, we restrict predictions to those with a "time" semantic type. This mapping process effectively serves as a pruning mechanism for BARQA’s span predictions using gold/system mention boundaries and semantic metadata. Tables 3 and 4 summarize the performance of BARQA relative to previous work on ISNotes and BASHI, respectively. In these experiments, BARQA is trained using the optimal strategy from Table 2: pre-training on QuasiBridging followed by fine-tuning on a small amount of in-domain data.
source_text: Previous work for bridging anaphora resolution on ISNotes and BASHI use gold/system mentions as antecedent candidates and report results using strict accuracy (Hou et al., 2013b; Hou, 2018a) . In order to fairly compare against these systems, for every bridging anaphor a, we first map all top 20 span predictions of our system BARQA to the gold/system mentions, then we choose the gold/system mention with the highest confidence score as the predicted antecedent. Specifically, we map a predicted span s to a mention m if they share the same head and s is part of m (m is created by removing all postmodifiers from m). For instance, "total potential claims" is mapped to the mention "the total potential claims from the disaster". If a predicted span can not be mapped to any gold/system mentions, we filter it out. Following Hou (2018a), we only keep the predictions whose semantic types are "time" if a is a time expression. The above process is equal to using gold/system mentions and their semantic information to further prune BARQA's span predictions. Table 3 and Table 4 compare the results of our system BARQA against previous studies for bridging anaphora resolution on ISNotes and BASHI, respectively. For both datasets, the BARQA model is trained using the best strategy reported in Table 2 (pre-training with QuasiBridging + fine-tuning with small in-domain data).

text: Formally, we define a translation memory (TM) as a collection of $K$ bilingual pairs $\{(s_k, t_k)\}_{k=1}^K$, where $s_k$ and $t_k$ represent mutual translations. To ensure computational efficiency, the TM must be structured for rapid retrieval of the pair $(s_k, t_k)$ that maximizes the similarity between $s_k$ and a given query sentence. Existing methodologies for measuring sentence similarity typically bifurcate into lexical matching—often referred to as fuzzy matching—and distributional semantics. While the former focuses on the extent of discrete overlap between sentences, the latter exploits the generalization capacity of neural networks to generate latent vector representations. We proceed by detailing the specific similarity measures adopted in this research.
source_text: More formally, we consider a TM as a set of K sentence pairs {(s k , t k ) ∶ k = 1, . . . , K} where s k and t k are mutual translations. A TM must be conveniently stored so as to allow fast access to the pair (s k , t k ) that shows the highest similarity between s k and any given new sentence. Many methods to compute sentence similarity have been explored, mainly falling into two broad categories: lexical matches (i.e. fuzzy match) and distributional semantics. The former relies on the number of overlaps between the sentences taken into account. The latter counts on the generalisation power of neural networks when building vector representations. Next, we describe the similarity measures employed in this work.

text: It is important to observe that superior similarity models are readily accessible, especially considering that existing methods predate recent transformative breakthroughs in pretraining (Devlin et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). Additionally, the paraphrase dataset employed for encoder training is derived from the SNLI corpus (Bowman et al., 2015) and augmented with unsupervised multi-task data from sources including Wikipedia and digital news. While such extensive coverage likely ensures robust performance across diverse domains, the unique linguistic characteristics of interactive fiction (IF) and its sub-components, such as dialogue, would likely benefit from specialized domain-specific fine-tuning.
source_text: We note that significantly better similarity models are easily attainable as predates recent breakthrough techniques in pretraining (Devlin et al., 2018) (Yang et al., 2019) . Furthermore, the dataset of paraphrase pairs used to train the encoder of is drawn from the SNLI corpus (Bowman et al., 2015) with additional unsupervised multi-task training data taken from sources such as Wikipedia and web news; this broad coverage will likely give reasonable results for any domain, but the specific flavor of IF or its subtypes (e.g. dialouge) would likely benefit from domain specific fine-tuning.

text: Microsoft MT-DNN is an open-source natural language understanding toolkit designed to assist researchers and developers in building customized deep learning models. Its primary features include: (1) support for robust and transferable learning via an adversarial multi-task learning paradigm, and (2) the enablement of knowledge distillation within a multi-task framework, which can be leveraged to produce streamlined models for efficient online deployment. In the future, we will extend MT-DNN to support natural language generation tasks, such as question generation, and incorporate additional pre-trained encoders like T5 (Raffel et al., 2019).
source_text: Microsoft MT-DNN is an open-source natural language understanding toolkit which facilitates researchers and developers to build customized deep learning models. Its key features are: 1) support for robust and transferable learning using adversarial multi-task learning paradigm; 2) enable knowledge distillation under the multi-task learning setting which can be leveraged to derive lighter models for efficient online deployment. We will extend MT-DNN to support Natural Language Generation tasks, e.g. Question Generation, and incorporate more pre-trained encoders, e.g. T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) in future.

text: Sample headlines from Common Crawl, such as "As a New Emperor Is Enthroned in Japan, His Wife Won't Be Allowed to Watch," "Japanese Emperor Akihito to abdicate after three decades on throne," "Japan's Emperor Akihito says he is abdicating as of Tuesday at a ceremony, in his final official address to his people," and "Akihito begins abdication rituals as Japan marks end of era," illustrate instances involving large, automatically aggregated collections of news articles centered on specific events. These scenarios encompass news event detection, news article retrieval, and timeline generation. Given the ubiquity of such applications, there is an urgent requirement for improved datasets tailored to these multi-document summarization (MDS) use cases.
