This includes news event detection, news article search, and timeline generation. Given the prevalence of such applications, there is a pressing need for better datasets for these MDS use cases.

text: Lastly, the trends depicted in Figure 4 suggest that no agent achieves total convergence within the training budget, even as RE and FLE reach a plateau. Although DQN might eventually converge to the Rule DM’s performance ceiling given substantial additional steps, our proposed methods exhibit significantly greater sample efficiency. Table 1 (bottom) provides the evaluation results for DQfD agents employing RoFL fine-tuning. All weak experts show improvement under RoFL, most notably RLE, which sees an 8% increase in Success Rate. We also report the performance of the final fine-tuned FFN classifier; its 15% Success Rate improvement over the base model indicates that fine-tuning effectively bridges the domain gap between the offline data and the RL environment.
source_text: Finally, the graphs in Figure 4 indicate that none of the agents fully converge after the training step limit, although RE and FLE plateau. It is possible that after significantly more steps even DQN would converge to the ceiling performance of the Rule DM -but all our methods are considerably more sample efficient. Table 1 (bottom) shows evaluation results of DQfD agents trained with RoFL fine-tuning. All weak experts improve with RoFL, especially the RLE which records an 8% jump in Success Rate. We also include the performance of the final fine-tuned FFN classifier, whose improvement over its original incarnation (15% higher Success Rate) demonstrates that fine-tuning helps narrow the domain gap between data and the RL environment.

text: Our framework supports language modeling (LM) and neural text-to-speech (TTS) in addition to automatic speech recognition (ASR), speech translation (ST), and machine translation (MT) tasks. To the best of our knowledge, no other toolkit supports all of these tasks within a single framework. A comparison with existing frameworks is summarized in Table 1. While it is conceptually possible to combine ASR and MT modules for cascaded speech translation (Cascade-ST), few toolkits provide practical examples of such integration. Furthermore, although some frameworks support speech-to-text tasks, transitioning between ASR and end-to-end speech translation (E2E-ST) is often non-trivial, as E2E-ST typically requires auxiliary ASR or MT objectives to achieve competitive performance.
source_text: We support language modeling (LM), neural textto-speech (TTS) in addition to ASR, ST, and MT tasks. To the best of our knowledge, none of frameworks support all these tasks in a single toolkit. A comparison with other frameworks are summarized in Table 1 . Conceptually, it is possible to combine ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST, but few frameworks provide such examples. Moreover, though some toolkits indeed support speechto-text tasks, it is not trivial to switch ASR and E2E-ST tasks since E2E-ST requires the auxiliary tasks (ASR/MT objectives) to achieve reasonable performance.

text: Our experiments are conducted across two distinct benchmarks. We utilize the Multi30K dataset (Elliott et al., 2016) to evaluate the performance of the PLuGS model on a standard resource widely adopted by the research community. Our focus is on Task 1 for French as described by Caglayan et al. (2019), which involves generating a French translation using an image and an English caption as inputs. The training set is composed of images from the Flickr30K training and validation partitions, paired with their associated French captions. The validation split contains the test2016 images and captions, while the test split consists of the test2017 images and captions.
source_text: We perform our experiments using two different benchmarks. We use the Multi30K (Elliott et al., 2016) dataset in order to compare the effect of the PLuGS model using a resource that has been widely used in the community. We focus on Task 1 for French from (Caglayan et al., 2019) , generating a translation in French based on an image and an English caption as input. The training set consists of images from the Flickr30K train and validation splits, along with the corresponding French captions. The validation split consists of test2016 images and captions, and the test split consists of the test2017 images and captions.

text: The source material for these puzzles is primarily distributed in PDF format. A significant majority of the languages represented in the dataset utilize the Latin alphabet, often supplemented by diacritical marks. For languages that traditionally employ non-Latin scripts or lack a formal writing system, the data is rendered through International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) transcriptions or Latin-based transliterations. Georgian represents the sole exception in the corpus that retains a non-Latin orthography, specifically the Mkhedruli script. Given the heterogeneity of puzzle types featured in the Olympiads, we isolated tasks pertinent to our research by automatically filtering for the keywords "translation" and "matching," subsequently performing manual verification to confirm the relevance of the results.
source_text: The puzzles are generally provided as pdf files. Many languages in the dataset use the Latin script, optionally with some diacritics. Some which use a non-Latin script (or have no writing system at all), are transcribed with IPA or transliterated into the Latin script. Only one language, Georgian, uses a non-Latin script, namely the Mkhedruli script. As there are various types of puzzles presented at the Olympiads, we identified the ones relevant to our task through automatic filtering for the keywords "translation" or "matching", and manually verified the results.

text: Finally, we observe that utilizing translations from online MT systems surpasses reference-based evaluation, with these discrepancies being more pronounced for the Estonian-English language pair. This may be attributed to the superior quality of pseudo-references in into-English translation, which renders them as effective as gold-standard references while remaining more similar to the evaluated MT output. In the case of English-Czech, pseudo-references align more closely with mt-ref and typically underperform compared to hyp-mt-avg-ref. Table 3 demonstrates the benefits of our uncertainty-aware evaluation approach relative to traditional reference-based metrics. We present MC dropout and top beam hypotheses for both high-quality and low-quality MT outputs. First, it is noteworthy that MC dropout hypotheses exhibit significant diversity for low-quality outputs but remain relatively consistent for high-quality translations. Conversely, beam hypotheses remain largely uniform across both quality levels. Second, scores derived from MC dropout hypotheses clearly distinguish between low- and high-quality translations; in contrast, Meteor yields a higher score for the low-quality sample because of lexical and synonym overlaps that, in this instance, do not accurately reflect translation quality.
source_text: Finally, we observe that using translations from online MT systems also outperforms referencebased evaluation. The differences are larger for Estonian-English. This could be because for into-English translation the quality of pseudo-references is higher, making them as good as actual reference translations, while yet closer to the MT output under evaluation. For English-Czech, pseudoreferences are closer to mt-ref and generally worse than hyp-mt-avg-ref. Table 3 illustrates the advantage of our uncertainty-aware evaluation over standard reference-based scoring. We show MC dropout and top beam hypotheses for a high quality and for a low quality MT output. First, note that MC dropout hypotheses are very different for a low-quality MT output and fairly similar for good-quality translation. By contrast, beam hypotheses are similar or the same in both cases. Second, the evaluation scores obtained using MC dropout hypotheses result in a large difference between low-quality and high-quality MT outputs, whereas Meteor assigns a higher score to the low-quality example due to surface word and synonym matches that are in this case not indicative of MT quality.

text: Finally, we evaluated our model against Quagga, the state-of-the-art neural segmentation framework by Repke and Krestel, alongside a re-implementation of the SVM-based email cleaning method by Tang et al. Due to the absence of a training routine in the Quagga source code, we re-implemented this component as faithfully as possible, with the notable exception of quotation handling. Whereas the original model lacked a quotation class and ignored markers to predict content within quoted text—making conversation reconstruction from segments alone impossible—our approach classifies quotations as distinct segments. This preserves the structural integrity of emails, allowing for the removal of quotation indicators and subsequent recursive model application. We trained our implementation of Quagga on all 16 classes for 20 epochs, as further training led to overfitting. Although the original Quagga was designed for only five classes, our extended version reached 94% accuracy, performing only slightly worse than our model with comparable scores for most frequent classes. However, the performance degradation on the Enron corpus was more significant for Quagga than for our model, except within the log data class. Ultimately, both models perform well, yet our approach achieves better overall generalization. Furthermore, our model is faster and more efficient to train, as it utilizes a 2D context window rather than a vertical RNN for processing line sequences.
source_text: Finally, we compared our model against Quagga, the state-of-the-art neural segmentation model by Repke and Krestel and a re-implementation of Tang et al.'s SVM email cleaning approach. Unfortunately, a training routine was missing from Quagga's source code, so we re-implemented this part as closely to the original as possible with one notable exception. We changed the way the model handles quotations. The original model did not have a quotation class and was instead trained to ignore quotation indicators so as to predict normal content segments within quotations also. This is very different from how our model handles quotations and it renders the reconstruction of a conversation from the segments alone impossible. We prefer our approach to classify quotations as a separate segment, which retains the structure of emails and one can simply strip the quotation indicators and then apply the model recursively. We trained our own Quagga on all 16 classes for 20 epochs (the model started overfitting after more epochs). Although the original model was trained and tested on only five classes, the extended and retrained model performs only slightly worse than ours with 94% accuracy overall and very similar scores for most of the frequent classes. The degradation on the Enron corpus appears to be worse than in our model (with the exception of the log data class). In conclusion, we can say that both models perform equally well, though our model achieves overall better generalization. In terms of training speed, we found our approach to be faster and more efficient, since it relies on a 2D context window instead of a vertical RNN for sequences of lines.

text: Drawing upon the work of Strobelt et al. (2017, 2018), EXBERT facilitates nearest neighbor searches of embeddings within a reference corpus through the following procedure. The corpus is first segmented by sentence, and its tokens are labeled with specific metadata, such as POS, DEP, and NER. Subsequently, the model processes this corpus, and its embeddings $E^{(l)}$ are stored for every layer $l$ and indexed for Cosine Similarity (CS) search using faiss (Johnson et al., 2019). The top 50 tokens most similar to a query embedding are then displayed and summarized for the user within the context of their occurrence in the annotated corpus.
source_text: Searching Inspired by Strobelt et al. (2017 Strobelt et al. ( , 2018 , EXBERT performs a nearest neighbor search of embeddings on a reference corpus as follows. A corpus is first split by sentence and its tokens labeled for desired metadata (e.g., POS, DEP, NER). The model then processes this corpus, and its embeddings E (l) are stored at every layer l and indexed for a Cosine Similarity (CS) search using faiss (Johnson et al., 2019) . The top 50 most similar tokens matching a query embedding are displayed and summarized for the user in the context of their use in the annotated corpus.

text: Previous research has employed high-level utterance representations, such as latent variables or keywords, to facilitate policy learning (He et al., 2018; Yao et al., 2018). However, these utterance-level methods often result in less coherent multi-turn dialogues, as learning semantic transitions within a conversational flow is difficult when relying solely on dialogue data without the assistance of prior information. In this paper, we propose representing prior information regarding dialogue transitions (between a message and its response) as a graph and optimizing the dialogue policy based on this graph to promote more coherent dialogues.
source_text: high-level utterance representations, e.g., latent variables or keywords, to facilitate policy learning (He et al., 2018; Yao et al., 2018; . However, these utterance-level methods tend to produce less coherent multi-turn dialogs since it is quite challenging to learn semantic transitions in a dialog flow merely from dialog data without the help of prior information. In this paper, we propose to represent prior information about dialog transition (between a message and its response) as a graph, and optimize dialog policy based on the graph, to foster a more coherent dialog.

text: Across all problem domains, both the S-DAP and M-DAP models exceed the performance of the SVM-BoW baseline, suggesting that the implemented DAP architecture is appropriate for this task. However, the results indicate that M-DAP performs on par with S-DAP. This finding suggests that the information integrated by the coherence model does not benefit the dialogue act prediction task. While the coherence model identifies semantic relationships by encoding the content of utterances, dialogue acts—which reflect the communicative intentions of speakers—are independent of such content; consequently, the information extracted by the coherence model does not improve DAP performance. Nevertheless, as other experiments in this study demonstrate, dialogue acts can assist in producing more informative utterance representations for modeling dialogue coherence. Ultimately, our multi-task learning approach mitigates the necessity for explicit dialogue act labels in coherence assessment, which represents the primary goal of this work.
source_text: Both S-DAP and M-DAP models outperform the SVM-BoW model for all problem-domains, indi- cating that the employed DAP model is suitable for solving this task. However, we observe that the M-DAP model works on par with the S-DAP model. This observation shows that the information encoded by the coherence model is not useful for solving the dialogue act prediction task. The coherence model captures semantic relations in a dialogue by encoding information about the content of utterances. Dialogue acts, which indicate speakers' intentions of stating utterances in a dialogue, are independent of the content of utterances, therefore information learned by the coherence model does not help the DAP model. However, as the other experiments in this paper demonstrate, DAs can help to obtain more informative utterance representations to model dialogue coherence. Our multi-task learning approach relieves the need for explicit DA labels for coherence assessments, which is the main goal of this paper.

text: Despite their diverse characteristics, these methodologies are uniformly founded upon Transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017) and necessitate a high-quality AR model to generate the translation outputs that serve as training targets for the NAR model. This specific training configuration is highly amenable to the integration of external monolingual data, as the target components of the NAR training set are invariably synthesized by an AR model. While approaches like backtranslation (Sennrich et al., 2015a) are recognized for improving MT results using solely monolingual resources, to our knowledge, the use of monolingual data augmentation for NAR-MT has not yet been documented in the literature.
source_text: Although different in various aspects, all of these methods are based on transformer modules (Vaswani et al., 2017) , and depend on a well-trained AR model to obtain its output translations to create targets for NAR model training. This training setup is well-suited to leverage external monolingual data, since the target side of the NAR training corpus is always generated by an AR model. Techniques like backtranslation (Sennrich et al., 2015a) are known to improve MT performance using monolingual data alone. However, to the best of our knowledge, monolingual data augmentation for NAR-MT has not been reported in the literature.

text: Elucidating the internal representations of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) remains a formidable challenge, prompting the development of various probing methodologies to investigate their linguistic encoding. Direct approaches include extracting finite-state automata (e.g., Weiss et al., 2017) from the model or analyzing hidden state dynamics during inference to identify specific neurons sensitive to particular features (e.g., Shi et al., 2016; Linzen et al., 2016; Tenney et al., 2019). Alternatively, researchers may employ behavioral tasks that necessitate syntactic competence—such as grammaticality judgments or number agreement—to infer the presence of structural representations based on the model's output (Linzen et al., 2016; Ettinger et al., 2018; Gulordava et al., 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019).
source_text: Uncovering what an RNN actually represents is notoriously difficult, and several methods for probing RNNs' linguistic representations have been developed to approach the problem. Most directly, one can extract finite automata (e.g., Weiss et al., 2017) from the network or measure its state as it processes inputs to determine which neurons attend to what features (e.g., Shi et al., 2016; Linzen et al., 2016; Tenney et al., 2019) . Alternatively, one can present a task which only a syntactic model should be able to solve, such as grammaticality discrimination or an agreement task, and then infer if a model has syntactic representations based on its behavior (Linzen et al., 2016; Ettinger et al., 2018; Gulordava et al., 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019) .

text: Our methods are initially applied to English (En) paired with German (De), French (Fr), and Russian (Ru) using sample data from the BUCC corpus. We develop individual translation models for each source language into English. All models employ the Transformer-Base architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) and are trained via Marian (Junczys-Dowmunt et al., 2018) with BPE. We source parallel data from the WMT news translation task (Bojar et al., 2015), though we exclude News Commentary to prevent the models from memorizing gold parallel sentences due to overlap concerns.
source_text: We apply our methods on English (En) paired with German (De), French (Fr) and Russian (Ru) on BUCC sample data initially. We train separate translation models for each language into English. All models are Transformer-Base (Vaswani et al., 2017) , trained using Marian (Junczys-Dowmunt et al., 2018) with BPE applied. We use parallel data from WMT news translation task (Bojar et al., 2015) , excluding News Commentary to prevent our systems from memorising the gold parallel sentences given the overlap issue.

text: Following a rigorous post-processing stage (detailed in Appendix A.2), we produced a dataset containing 13,667 sentences harvested from 50% of the Common-Crawl corpus. To verify the quality of the resulting data, we manually annotated three random samples of 100 sentences each; the identification of authentic NArabizi instances confirmed a corpus accuracy of 97%. The evaluation results for the two classification paradigms, conducted on the development and test partitions of the baseline NArabizi corpus, are detailed in Table 2. Empirical results indicate that the fastText architecture, utilizing n-gram features, achieves greater precision than the non-neural model relying on language-identification feature vectors.
source_text: After an intensive post-processing step (cf. Appendix A.2), this process results in a dataset of 13,667 sentences extracted from half the Common-Crawl corpus. 7 To evaluate the quality of the resulting data set, we randomly picked 3 times 100 sentences, and genuine NArabizi sentences were manually identified, which allowed us to assess the accuracy of our corpus as reaching 97%. Table 2 presents the results of the evaluation of the two classification methods performed on both the development and test sets of the original NArabizi corpus. 8 Results show that the fastText classifier and its n-gram features is more precise than its nonneural counterpart and its language-id feature vectors.

text: We evaluated the efficacy of our model through a document retrieval task designed to identify the full body of a parent paper using its abstract as the search query. For this experiment, we randomly selected 10,000 abstracts from the database and retrieved the 20 most similar candidate papers for each. We assessed performance by measuring the frequency with which the ground-truth parent paper appeared at the first rank or within the top 20 results. Our model correctly identified the parent paper as the top result in 83.1% of cases, surpassing the 71.0% accuracy achieved by a Sent2Vec model pre-trained on Wikipedia (Pagliardini et al., 2018). Additionally, our model included the target paper within the top 20 results for 95.1% of trials, compared to 87.0% for the Wikipedia-based Sent2Vec baseline. This enhanced retrieval capability likely stems from training on a domain-specific corpus characterized by specialized terminology and rare vocabulary (Roy et al., 2017; Blagec et al., 2019). Consequently, these results suggest that the model requires periodic retraining to adapt as the database expands to include documents from diverse or novel domains.
source_text: To test the performance of our model, we performed experiments on a simple text retrieval task. The goal of this task was to retrieve the full body of a parent paper given its abstract as the search query. We randomly sampled 10000 abstracts from the database and retrieved the 20 most similar papers for each abstract. As an evaluation metric, we counted the fraction of retrievals in which the parent paper appeared on top or among the top 20 results. Our model retrieved the correct parent paper as the top search result in 83.1% of the trials, compared to 71.0% when using a Sent2Vec model trained on Wikipedia (Pagliardini et al., 2018) . Furthermore, the parent paper was among the top 20 retrievals in 95.1% of cases when using our model, compared to 87.0% for the Wikipedia Sent2Vec model. The higher retrieval performance of our model in this task likely arises from its training on a domain-specific corpus that contains rare words and terminologies (Roy et al., 2017; Blagec et al., 2019) . This suggests that the model would need to be retrained at regular intervals, particularly when papers from other domains are added to the database.

text: To identify non-entailment pairs within the dataset, we develop a binary classifier to determine whether a source document entails its corresponding headline. Given that pretrained language models like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) have recently achieved significant advancements in recognizing textual entailment (RTE), we fine-tune such models on supervised data regarding the entailment relationship between source documents and headlines. For the English Gigaword dataset, we utilize a pretrained RoBERTa-large model fine-tuned on Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference (MultiNLI) (Williams et al., 2018). We then perform additional fine-tuning on supervision data consisting of lead-headline pairs with entailment labels (as detailed in Section 3). This supervision set includes lead-headline pairs where two or three human annotators assigned labels of either entail or non-entail, while other pairs were omitted. Following 10 epochs of fine-tuning on the RoBERTa model, we produced a binary entailment classifier with 91.7% accuracy on a hold-out evaluation (761 training and 179 test instances). For the JNC dataset, we employ the Japanese-specific pretrained BERT model (Kikuta, 2019). Because a large-scale Japanese semantic inference corpus comparable to MultiNLI is unavailable, we developed supervision data for the entailment relationship between the initial three sentences and headlines (hereafter lead3-headline) within the JNC. We extracted 12,000 lead3-headline pairs from the JNC and acquired entailment labels through crowdsourcing, where each pair was evaluated by five workers. Only lead3-headline pairs receiving four or five consistent labels of either entail or non-entail were included in the supervision data. The resulting entailment classifier fine-tuned on this data achieved 83.9% accuracy on a hold-out evaluation comprising 5,033 training and 1,678 test instances.
source_text: In order to find non-entailment pairs in the dataset, we build a binary classifier that judges whether a source document entails its headline or not. Recently, pretrained language models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) show remarkable advances in the task of recognizing textual entailment (RTE) 8 . Thus, we fine-tune pretrained models on the supervision data for entailment relation between source documents and their headlines. For English Gigaword dataset, we use the pretrained RoBERTa large finetuned on Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference (MultiNLI) (Williams et al., 2018) . We further fine-tuned the model on the supervision data of the leadheadline pairs with entailment labels (acquired in Section 3). Here, the supervision data include leadheadline pairs where two or three human subjects labeled either entail or non-entail; other pairs were excluded from the supervision data. In this way, we obtained a binary classifier for entailment relation of 91.7% accuracy on a hold-out evaluation (761 training and 179 test instances) after running 10 epoch of fine-tuning on the RoBERTa model. For JNC, we use the pretrained BERT model for Japanese text (Kikuta, 2019) . However, no large-scale Japanese corpus for semantic inference (counterpart to MultiNLI) is available. Thus, we created supervision data for entailment relation between lead three sentences and headlines (lead3headline, hereafter) on JNC. We extracted 12,000 lead3-headline pairs from JNC, and collected entailment labels using crowdsourcing. Each pair had five entailment labels assigned by five crowd workers. We used lead3-headline pairs where four or five crowd workers labeled either entail or nonentail; other pairs were unused in the supervision data. The entailment classifier fine-tuned on the supervision data achieved 83.9% accuracy on a hold-out evaluation with 5,033 training and 1,678 test instances.

text: Building upon these considerations, we propose the Persona Perception Bot (P 2 BOT), which explicitly models the understanding between interlocutors through a transmitter-receiver framework. Unlike traditional methods, P 2 BOT introduces the novel concept of mutual persona perception, which more effectively captures the information exchange process that enables interlocutors to become acquainted. To train P 2 BOT for personalized dialogue generation, we employ supervised training and self-play fine-tuning guided by reward signals that characterize mutual persona perception. Experiments on the PERSONA-CHAT dataset demonstrate the superiority of our approach over baseline models across both automatic metrics and human evaluations.
source_text: Taking into account the above, we propose Persona Perception Bot (P 2 BOT), explicitly modeling the understanding between interlocutors with a transmitter-receiver framework. Distinguished from traditional methods, P 2 BOT highlights a novel concept, mutual persona perception, which is better suited to describe the information exchange process that empowers the interlocutors to get to know each other. In order to train P 2 BOT for personalized dialogue generation, we employ supervised training and self-play fine-tuning piloted by reward signals characterizing mutual persona perception. Experiments on the PERSONA-CHAT dataset demonstrate the superiority of our approach over the baselines in both automatic met-rics and human evaluations 1 .

text: Consequently, we evaluate two approaches to modeling narrative suspense: surprise, a backward-looking metric assessing the unexpectedness of the current state relative to the preceding narrative; and uncertainty reduction, a forward-looking metric evaluating the unexpectedness of future story developments. Each metric can be calculated either directly from story representations or indirectly through the probability distributions associated with those representations. We introduce a hierarchical language model utilizing Generative Pre-Training (GPT, Radford et al., 2018) to generate story-level representations, alongside an inference framework that leverages these representations to quantify both surprise and uncertainty reduction. For our evaluation, we utilize the WritingPrompt short story corpus (Fan et al., 2018), supplementing a portion of it with human-annotated, sentence-level suspense ratings. Our findings indicate that surprise calculated over both representations and probability distributions effectively predicts suspense levels. Nevertheless, uncertainty reduction applied to representations demonstrates superior performance, achieving accuracy levels comparable to human judgment. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our models are capable of identifying turning points—defined as significant narrative shifts—within movie synopses (Papalampidi et al., 2019).
source_text: We will therefore compare two ways for modelling narrative suspense: surprise, a backwardlooking measure of how unexpected the current state is given the story so far; and uncertainty reduction, a forward-looking and measure of how unexpected the continuation of the story is. Both measures can be computed either directly over story representations, or indirectly over the probability distributions over such representations. We propose a hierarchical language model based on Generative Pre-Training (GPT, Radford et al., 2018) to encode story-level representations and develop an inference scheme that uses these representations to compute both surprise and uncertainty reduction. For evaluation, we use the WritingPrompt corpus of short stories (Fan et al., 2018) , part of which we annotate with human sentence-by-sentence judgements of suspense. We find that surprise over representations and over probability distributions both predict suspense judgements. However uncertainty reduction over representations is better, resulting in near human-level accuracy. We also show that our models can be used to predict turning points, i.e., major narrative events, in movie synopses (Papalampidi et al., 2019) .

text: The majority of current Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models function at the word or subword level, which often leads to memory inefficiencies due to expansive vocabulary sizes. Conversely, character-level models (Lee et al., 2017; Cherry et al., 2018) process raw characters directly, yielding more compact linguistic representations while alleviating out-of-vocabulary (OOV) issues (Luong and Manning, 2016). Character-level approaches are also highly effective for multilingual translation, as several languages can be modeled using a single shared character vocabulary. Such multilingual training can enhance overall performance without increasing model complexity (Lee et al., 2017), while simultaneously obviating the need to develop separate models for each specific language pair.
source_text: Most existing Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models operate on the word or subword-level, which tends to make these models memory inefficient because of large vocabulary sizes. Characterlevel models (Lee et al., 2017; Cherry et al., 2018) instead work directly on raw characters, resulting in a more compact language representation, while mitigating out-of-vocabulary (OOV) problems (Luong and Manning, 2016) . Characterlevel models are also very suitable for multilingual translation since multiple languages can be modeled using the same character vocabulary. Multilingual training can lead to improvements in the overall performance without an increase in model complexity (Lee et al., 2017) , while also circumventing the need to train separate models for each language pair.

text: Modern deep learning methodologies for abstractive summarization inherently learn to incorporate information from training sets while producing summaries for specific documents (See et al., 2017; Gehrmann et al., 2018). More sophisticated pretrained text generation models (Radford et al., 2018, 2019; Dong et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019; Khandelwal et al., 2019; Rothe et al., 2020) exhibit an enhanced capacity for capturing world knowledge, as they are trained on vast quantities of background text. This capability is exemplified in Figure 1, where the input document does not explicitly state that the "London mayoral election" took place in "2016"; however, the summary produced by the pretrained GPT-TUNED model correctly identifies this detail, consistent with the human-authored reference. In this research, we support the claim that abstractive systems should synthesize background knowledge to create more substantive and informative summaries. More specifically, we argue that "hallucinations in summarization are acceptable if they lead to better summaries that are factual with respect to the document and the associated background knowledge." This perspective also allows for an investigation into the ability of current neural models to leverage background knowledge for the generation of factually grounded abstracts (see Section 5.3).
source_text: Recent deep learning approaches to abstractive summarization naturally learn to integrate knowledge from the training data while generating an abstractive summary for a document (See et al., 2017; Gehrmann et al., 2018) . More advanced pretrained text generators (Radford et al., 2018 (Radford et al., , 2019 Dong et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019; Khandelwal et al., 2019; Rothe et al., 2020) are even better at capturing world knowledge as they are informed by a vast amount of background text. This can be observed in the example shown in Figure 1 as the input document does not mention that the discussed "London mayoral election" is from "2016"; but the abstract generated by the pretrained text generator GPT-TUNED correctly predicts this information similar to the human-authored abstract. 2 In this paper we stand in favour of the assertion that abstractive systems may integrate with the background knowledge to generate rich and meaningful summaries. More concretely, "hallucinations in summarization are acceptable if they lead to better summaries that are factual with respect to the document and the associated background knowledge." This assumption also allows us to assess the capability of recent neural models to integrate with the background knowledge to generate factual abstracts (see Section 5.3).

text: Prior to detailing the primary methodology, it should be noted that this research does not address the task of normalizing extracted spans into their concrete values; this objective can be effectively achieved via rule-based systems following the span extraction phase for categories such as times, dates, and party sizes. Hierarchical rule-based parsing engines, such as Duckling, currently exist to facilitate the parsing of complex temporal expressions like "the day after next Tuesday." Furthermore, phrases such as "Me and my wife and 2 kids" can be parsed with high precision by evaluating singular noun and numerical counts within the extracted span.
source_text: Before we delve into describing the core methodology, we note that in this work we are not concerned with the task of normalizing extracted spans to their actual values: this can be solved effectively with rule-based systems after the span extraction step for cases such as times, dates, and party sizes. There exist hierarchical rule-based parsing engines (e.g., Duckling) that allow for parsing times and dates such as "the day after next Tuesday". Further, phrases such as "Me and my wife and 2 kids" can be parsed using singular noun and number counts in the span with high precision.

text: Consistent with established weak supervision paradigms, labeling functions facilitate the integration of domain expertise into sequence labeling architectures. This capability is particularly vital in low-resource or zero-shot settings where labeled data is scarce (Hu et al., 2016; Wang and Poon, 2018). The framework enables the seamless addition of new functions to exploit diverse knowledge repositories for any given text domain. Moreover, labeling functions frequently exhibit cross-domain portability, offering a distinct advantage over manual annotations, which must be painstakingly recreated for every new target domain.
source_text: As in other weak supervision frameworks, the labelling functions allow us to inject expert knowledge into the sequence labelling model, which is often critical when data is scarce or non-existent (Hu et al., 2016; Wang and Poon, 2018) . New la-belling functions can be easily inserted to leverage the knowledge sources at our disposal for a given textual domain. Furthermore, labelling functions can often be ported across domains, which is not the case for manual annotations that must be reiterated for every target domain.

text: This section evaluates the performance of models trained on Conceptual Captions, as detailed in Section 4. Table 2 presents the results on the OID1k test set for side-by-side (SxS) human evaluations, comparing the PLuGS model against the TGT model (upper half) and the TTG model (lower half). Across all five languages, the results demonstrate that the PLuGS model consistently outperforms the TGT model on both the Gain SxS and Gain Accept metrics. Specifically, the absolute Gain SxS improvements for PLuGS relative to TGT range from 3% to 5%, while the gains relative to TTG range from 1% to 3%, with similar trends reflected in the Gain Accept metric. Table 3 reports the CIDEr scores for the Conceptual Captions v1.1 (CC-1.1) validation set. Notably, the CIDEr metric fails to exhibit any meaningful correlation with the results of the SxS human evaluations.
source_text: In this section, we evaluate the performance of models trained using Conceptual Captions, as detailed in Sec. 4. Table 2 presents the results on the OID1k testset for the SxS human evaluations between the TGT and PLuGS models (upper half), and between the TTG and PLuGS models (lower half). The results show that, for all five languages, the PLuGS model captions are consistently superior to the TGT captions on both Gain SxS and Gain Accept metrics. The Gain SxS are between 3% and 5% absolute percentages between TGT and PLuGS models, and 1% and 3% absolute percentages between TTG and PLuGS models, with similar trends for the Gain Accept metric. Table 3 presents the CIDEr scores on the validation set of the Conceptual Captions v1.1 (CC-1.1). The CIDEr metric fails to capture any meaningful correlation between its scores and the results of the SxS human evaluations.

text: Our framework incorporates end-to-end text-to-speech (E2E-TTS) capabilities, which can be applied to the translations produced by the ST system. The E2E-TTS architecture comprises a feature generation network that maps input text to acoustic representations—such as log-mel filterbank coefficients—and a vocoder network that synthesizes these features into a final waveform. For the feature generation stage, we support Tacotron 2 (Shen et al., 2018), Transformer-TTS, FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019), and their respective extensions, including multi-speaker models. Available vocoder options include WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) and Parallel WaveGAN. For a more exhaustive discussion, we refer readers to Hayashi et al. (2020).
source_text: Text-to-speech (TTS) We also support end-toend text-to-speech (E2E-TTS), which can be applied after ST outputs a translation. The E2E-TTS model consists of the feature generation network converting an input text to acoustic features (e.g., log-mel filterbank coefficients) and the vocoder network converting the features to a waveform. Tacotron 2 (Shen et al., 2018) , Transformer-TTS , FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019) , and their variants such as a multi-speaker model are supported as the feature generation network. WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) and Parallel WaveGAN are available as the vocoder network. See Hayashi et al. (2020) for more details.

text: Consequently, the introduced corpus represents the most extensive publicly accessible dataset for stance detection using user-generated content currently available. In contrast to the crowd-sourced methodologies employed by Mohammad et al. (2016a) and Inkpen et al. (2017) PHEME, our dataset was annotated exclusively by highly proficient domain experts. Furthermore, whereas previous stance detection research has focused on a restricted set of predominantly political topics, this work broadens the scope of investigated domains through the inclusion of a new financial dataset. Consequently, the WT-WT dataset provides a high-quality and rigorous benchmark for the research community to train and evaluate model performance and scalability, as well as to explore domain adaptation. Its substantial size also enables the pre-training of models before they are applied to domains characterized by data scarcity.
source_text: This makes the proposed dataset by far the largest publicly available dataset for stance detection on user-generated data. In contrast with Mohammad et al. (2016a), Inkpen et al. (2017) PHEME, where crowd-sourcing was used, only highly skilled domain experts were involved in the annotation process of our dataset. Moreover, previous work on stance detection focused on a relatively narrow range of mainly political topics: in this work, we widen the spectrum of considered domains in the stance detection research with a new financial dataset. For these reasons, the WT-WT dataset constitutes a high quality and robust benchmark for the research community to train and compare performance of models and their scalability, as well as for research on domain adaptation. Its large size also allows for pre-trainining of models, before moving to domain with data-scarcity.

text: The evaluation employs a 1-to-4 scale: a score of 1 indicates that the response is satisfactory only in terms of grammar and sentence structure; 2 signifies that, beyond being grammatically correct, the response is contextually coherent; 3 denotes a coherent response that is also engaging and informative, rather than a generic reply such as "Yes"; and 4 means the response aligns with the interlocutor’s persona, which is critical for assessing whether the model effectively utilizes persona-related information. As presented in Table 2, these findings are consistent with the automatic evaluation metrics, highlighting the superiority of P2BOT over the baseline methods. We also performed Wilcoxon signed-rank tests comparing our model to the baselines, with results confirming that the improvements are statistically significant at p < 0.05.
source_text: to 4. 1 means the response is good only in terms of grammar and sentence structure; 2 means in addition to valid grammar, the response is also coherent with the context; 3 means the coherent response is meanwhile interesting and informative, instead of just a simple response like "Yes"; And 4 means the response is consistent with the persona of the interlocutor, which is of extreme importance for the task of reflecting whether the model can effectively utilize the persona information. As shown in Table 2, the results are consistent with the automatic evaluation results, demonstrating the superiority of P 2 BOT against the baselines. We also conducted Wilcoxon signed-rank tests between our method and the baselines and the results show the improvements are significant with p < 0.05.

text: A significant challenge within multi-domain machine translation is the presence of cross-domain lexical ambiguity. For example, the term "article" exhibits distinct semantics in legal versus media contexts, requiring translation into Chinese as either "条款" (a specific legal clause) or "文章" (a journalistic piece). Our proposed word-level layer-wise domain mixing strategy seeks to mitigate this ambiguity. As outlined in Section 3.3, our architecture extracts varied representations for each token from its context across successive layers. Consequently, the domain distribution for each word evolves from the lower to the upper layers, ultimately facilitating the disambiguation of the target domain.
source_text: One major challenge in multi-domain machine translation is the word ambiguity in different domains. For example, the word "article" has different meanings in the domains of laws and media. When translating "article" into Chinese, the translated words are "条款" and "文章" , meaning a separate clause of a legal document and a piece of writing. Our proposed word-level layer-wise domain mixing approach tends to reduce the word ambiguity. As mentioned in Section 3.3, our model extracts different representations of each word from contexts at different layers. Accordingly, the domain proportion of each word evolves from bottom to top layers, and can eventually help identify the corresponding domains.

text: Step 2: Expert Annotation. Utilizing the aligned sentence groups from the professional and consumer versions of the MSD, we developed a specialized platform to facilitate expert annotation. Three medically trained physicians were tasked with selecting sentence pairs from these groups that share identical semantic meaning but differ in style. These experts possess formal medical training, qualifying them to accurately interpret medical semantics. To prevent subjective bias, annotators were not permitted to modify the text. Since the doctors are native Chinese speakers with English as a second language, we provided the English text alongside Chinese translations to improve annotation speed while maintaining high quality. Furthermore, an additional physician verified each pair of parallel sentences. Notably, these alignments are not restricted to one-to-one mappings; a single pairing may involve multiple professional or consumer sentences. This rigorous selection process led to the exclusion of many aligned groups, resulting in a final set of 675 test annotations, the medical PCIO element distribution of which is illustrated in Figure 3. Step 3: Knowledge Incorporation. To support knowledge-aware analysis, we utilize information extraction methods (Cao et al., 2018a, 2019) to identify medical concepts in each sentence. Specifically, we employ QuickUMLS (Soldaini and Goharian, 2016) to automatically link entity mentions to the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) (Bodenreider, 2004). For mentions referring to multiple concepts, we align them with the highest-ranked candidate. As indicated in the statistics for MSD and SimpWiki in Table 2, an individual annotation may comprise several sentences. Due to the high cost of expert labor, the MSD training set does not include parallel annotations. The layman-to-expert ratio for each metric represents the stylistic gap, where higher values indicate smaller differences, except in the case of sentence count (#Sentence).
source_text: Step 2: Expert Annotation. Given the aligned groups of sentences in professional and consumer MSD, we develop an annotation platform to facilitate expert annotations. We hire three doctors to select sentences from each version of group to annotate pairs of sentences that have the same meaning but are written in different styles. The hired doctors are formally medically trained, and are qualified to understand the semantics of the medical texts. To avoid subjective judgments in the annotations, they are not allowed to change the content. Particularly, the doctors are Chinese who also know English as a second language. Thus, we provide the English content accompanied with a Chinese translation as assistance, which helps to increase the annotation speed while ensuring quality. We also conduct verification on each pair of parallel sentences with the help of another doctor. Note that each pairing may contain multiple professional and consumer sentences; i.e., multiple alignment is possible, the alignments are not necessarily oneto-one. The strict procedure also discards many aligned groups, leading to 675 annotations for testing, with distribution of medical PCIO elements as shown in Figure 3 . Step 3: Knowledge Incorporation. To facilitate knowledge-aware analysis, we can utilize information extraction techniques (Cao et al., 2018a (Cao et al., , 2019 to identify medical concepts in each sentence. Here, we use QuickUMLS (Soldaini and Goharian, 2016) to automatically link entity mentions to Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) (Bodenreider, 2004) . Note that each mention may refer to multiple concepts, each for which we align to the highest ranked one. As shown in Table 2 : Statistics of MSD and SimpWiki. One annotation may contain multiple sentences, and MSD Train has no parallel annotations due to expensive expert cost. The ratio of layman to expert according to each metric denotes the gap between the two styles, and a higher value implies smaller differences except that for #Sentence.

text: Non-autoregressive (NAR) models (Oord et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017), which facilitate parallel token generation and significantly accelerate inference, have been extensively explored in natural language and speech processing domains such as neural machine translation (NMT) (Gu et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019b; Guo et al., 2019b), automatic speech recognition (ASR), and text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis (Oord et al., 2017). However, NAR models typically exhibit lower accuracy than their autoregressive (AR) counterparts because the internal dependencies among target tokens are explicitly removed.
source_text: Non-autoregressive (NAR) models (Oord et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017; , which generate all the tokens in a target sequence in parallel and can speed up inference, are widely explored in natural language and speech processing tasks such as neural machine translation (NMT) (Gu et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019b; Guo et al., 2019b) , automatic speech recognition (ASR) and text to speech (TTS) synthesis (Oord et al., 2017; . However, NAR models usually lead to lower accuracy than their autoregressive (AR) counterparts since the inner dependencies among the target tokens are explicitly removed.

text: The training framework, as depicted in Figure 1 (a), is centered on a mutual back-translation objective. Specifically, an anchored source sentence "$s_1 s_2 t s_3 t s_4$" is mapped to a target sequence "$t_1 t_2 t_3$" via source-to-target decoding; the resulting pair is then utilized to optimize the target-to-source translation model. Conversely, the target sentence "$t_1 t_2 t_3 t_4 t_5$" is translated into the anchored source "$s_1 s_2 s_3 t s_4$" through target-to-source decoding, forming a training pair for the source-to-target model. It should be noted that during this training phase, the model inputs are consistently pseudo-sentences generated by the MT system, whereas the target outputs are always ground-truth or anchored ground-truth sentences. In addition to this mutual back-translation scheme, we incorporate a denoising procedure standard in unsupervised MT (Lample et al., 2018b). This involves applying deletion and permutation noise to the source and target sentences, requiring the translation model to reconstruct the original, noise-free sequences.
source_text: The training process illustrated in Figure 1 (a) consists of a mutual back-translation procedure. The anchored source sentence "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 " is translated into target sentence "t 1 t 2 t 3 " by using source-to-target decoding, then "t 1 t 2 t 3 " and "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 " constitute a sentence pair for training the target-to-source translation model. In contrast, the target sentence "t 1 t 2 t 3 t 4 t 5 " is translated into anchored source sentence "s 1 s 2 s 3 t s 4 " by using target-to-source decoding, then both sen-tences constitute a sentence pair for training the source-to-target translation model. Note that during training the translation model, the input sentences are always pseudo sentences generated by decoding an MT model, while the output sentences are always true or anchored true sentences. Beside this mutual back-translation procedure, a denoising procedure used in unsupervised MT (Lample et al., 2018b ) is also adopted. The deletion and permutation noises are added to the source/target sentence, and the translation model is also trained to denoise them into the original source/target sentence.

text: Given the extensive datasets used for their training, pre-trained transformer language models are expected to generate text that is both fluent and diverse (See et al., 2019). It is noteworthy, however, that fine-tuned GPT-2 learns to produce output that is not only fluent but also adequate, despite its reliance on sequential representations of AMR graphs. As demonstrated in the experimental setup, the encoding of relations also plays a fundamental role in AMR-to-text performance, indicating that GPT-2 achieves a nuanced understanding of the underlying semantics to reach state-of-the-art results.
source_text: Due to the large amounts of data they are trained on, pre-trained transformer language models can be expected to generate fluent and diverse text (See et al., 2019) . It should however be highlighted that fine-tuned GPT-2 learns to produce not only fluent but also adequate text, despite using a sequential representation of an AMR graph as input. As shown in the experimental setup, encoding of relations plays as well a fundamental role in AMRto-text performance, indicating that GPT-2 attains a fine-grained understanding of the underlying semantics to reach state of the art performance.

text: Table 8 presents a sample document (ID: gakkai-e-0001014453 from ntc2-e1g) comparing author-provided keywords (gold) with various sets of automatically generated keyphrases. The gold standard includes: grammatical inference, knowledge acquisition, logic, progamming, and concept learning. The s2s+copy model produced: grammatical inference, knowledge, grammatical, knowledge representation, and natural language processing. Finally, mp-rank yielded: grammatical inference, documents, knowledge, large scale, and concept acquisition. Notably, the gold annotation contains a typographical error ("progamming").

text: The hidden states $h \uparrow k$ and $h \downarrow k$ are computed independently through bottom-up and top-down passes, respectively, where $h_k = TreeLSTM(h_k)$. The symbol $\oplus$ denotes the concatenation of these hidden states, and the resulting $h^{bi}_k$ serves as the final hierarchical representation for node $k$. To augment node representations using local graph structural data, we propose a Hierarchy-GCN based on the GCN framework (Kipf and Welling, 2017). While previous NLP research has refined Text-GCNs to produce rich word representations by leveraging syntactic structures and word correlations (Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017; Vashishth et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2018), we introduce a straightforward hierarchy-GCN specifically for the hierarchical structure to capture the aforementioned fine-grained hierarchical information.
source_text: where h ↑ k and h ↓ k are separately calculated in the bottom-up and top-down manner as h k = TreeLSTM( h k ). ⊕ indicates the concatenation of hidden states. The final hidden state of node k is the hierarchical node representation h bi k . Hierarchy-GCN GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017) is proposed to enhance node representations based on the local graph structural information. Some NLP studies have improved Text-GCNs for rich word representations upon the syntactic structure and word correlation (Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017; Vashishth et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2018) . We introduce a simple hierarchy-GCN for the hierarchy structure, thus gaining our aforementioned fine-grained hierarchy information.

text: Upon deriving the refined dependency parse trees $T_s$ for each sentence $s$, we construct a semantic graph by introducing edges between similar nodes across different trees. For any node pair $(v_i, v_j)$, a connection is established using a specialized SIMILAR edge type ($r_s$) provided the nodes meet the similarity criteria defined in Section A.1. Figure 4 illustrates representative examples of semantic graphs based on dependency parsing (DP) and semantic role labeling (SRL). Typically, DP-based graphs feature nodes with fewer words than SRL-based graphs, enabling the representation of more granular semantic relations. For example, while the SRL-based graph treats "a leading member of the Native American self-determination movement" as a single node, the DP-based graph represents it as a semantic triple: (*a leading member*, *pobj*, *the Native American self-determination movement*). This finer granularity often results in a sparser graph topology compared to the SRL-based version, which may potentially impede message passing during the graph propagation phase.
source_text: After obtaining the refined dependency parse tree T s for each sentence s, we add intra-tree edges to construct the semantic graph by connecting the nodes that are similar but from different parse trees. For each possible node pair v i , v j , we add an edge between them with a special edge type SIM-ILAR (denoted as r s ) if the two nodes are similar, i.e., satisfying the same condition as described in Section A.1. Figure 4 shows a real example for the DP-and SRL-based semantic graph, respectively. In general, DP-based graph contains less words for each node compared with the SRL-based graph, allowing it to include more fine-grained semantic relations. For example, a leading member of the Native American self-determination movement is treated as a single node in the SRL-based graph. While in the DP-based graph, it is represented as a semantic triple a leading member, pobj, the Native American self-determination movement . As the node is more fine-grained in the DP-based graph, this makes the graph typically more sparse than the SRL-based graph, which may hinder the message passing during graph propagation.

text: Following the methodology of Cai and Lam (2019), we employ a similar strategy to encode the incrementally expanding graph. Specifically, the graph is represented as a sequence of nodes (concepts) ordered chronologically by their time of insertion. We utilize a multi-layer Transformer architecture equipped with masked self-attention and source-attention; this setup ensures that each position in the node sequence attends only to itself and its predecessors, while allowing every node position to attend to the full input sequence. Although this design enables greater parallelization during training and efficient incrementality during inference, it inherently overlooks edge information. To mitigate this, we experimented with the approach of Strubell et al. (2018) by applying auxiliary supervision to the attention heads, encouraging them to focus on each node’s parents within the AMR graph. However, this did not result in performance gains. We attribute this outcome to the inherent capacity of neural attention mechanisms to learn relevant graph dependencies autonomously, suggesting that auxiliary supervision may instead disrupt the optimization of the primary parsing task.
source_text: We use a similar idea in Cai and Lam (2019) to encode the incrementally expanding graph. Specifically, a graph is simply treated as a sequence of nodes (concepts) in the chronological order of when they are inserted into the graph. We employ multi-layer Transformer architecture with masked self-attention and source-attention, which only allows each position in the node sequence to attend to all positions up to and including that position, and every position in the node sequence to attend over all positions in the input sequence. 3 While this design allows for significantly more parallelization during training and computation-saving incrementality during testing, 4 it inherently neglects the edge information. We attempted to alleviate this problem by incorporating the idea of Strubell et al. (2018) that applies auxiliary supervision at attention heads to encourage them to attend to each node's parents in the AMR graph. However, we did not see performance improvement. We attribute the failure to the fact that the neural attention mechanisms on their own are already capable of learning to attend to useful graph elements, and the auxiliary supervision is likely to disturb the ultimate parsing goal.

text: Chieu and Lee (2004) construct timelines by ranking sentences from a global document collection, where scores are derived from the aggregate similarity to other sentences within an $n$-day temporal window. Nguyen et al. (2014) introduced a pipeline-based approach to timeline generation that incorporates date selection, sentence clustering, and ranking. For the TLS task, Martschat and Markert (2018) adapted submodular function optimization—a method typically utilized in multi-document summarization. Their technique identifies an optimal combination of sentences across the entire corpus to build a timeline and currently defines the state-of-the-art for full TLS. Steen and Markert (2019) proposed a two-stage framework that decouples timeline construction into date selection followed by date-specific summarization. Furthermore, automated timeline generation has been investigated within the social media domain, where microblogging data is frequently clustered before being summarized (Wang et al., 2014; Li and Cardie, 2014). We adopt a similar framework to evaluate clustering-based TLS.
source_text: Chieu and Lee (2004) produce timelines by ranking sentences from an entire document collection. The ranking is based on summed up similarities to other sentences in an n-day window. Nguyen et al. (2014) propose a pipeline to generate timelines consisting of date selection, sentence clustering, and ranking. Martschat and Markert (2018) adapt submodular function optimization, commonly used for multi-document summarization, for the TLS task. The approach searches for a combination of sentences from a whole document collection to construct a timeline and is the current state-ofthe-art for full TLS. Steen and Markert (2019) use a two-stage approach consisting of date selection and date summarization to build timelines. Other examples of automatic timeline generation can be found in the social media-related literature, where microblogs are often clustered before being summarized (Wang et al., 2014; Li and Cardie, 2014) . We explore a similar framework for evaluating clustering-based TLS.

text: The ROUGE metric was originally introduced in the early 2000s to evaluate summary relevance, a period when extractive summarization served as the primary paradigm. Table 5 details the performance results on our test set, specifically addressing summary truthfulness. We report full-length ROUGE F1 scores, including R-1 (ROUGE-1), R-2 (ROUGE-2), and R-L (ROUGE-L). Within these results, "Sup" signifies the support score, while "Entail" denotes the proportion of outputs for which the entailment classifier (developed in Section 4.1) predicted an entailment relationship. Finally, "Truthful" represents the percentage of headlines that human evaluators judged to be factually accurate.
source_text: The ROUGE metric was proposed to measure the relevance of a summary when extractive summarization was the central approach (in the early 2000s). Obviously, the truthfulness of summaries Table 5 : Results on the test set. We used F1 full-length ROUGE score: R-1 (ROUGE-1), R-2 (ROUGE-2), and R-L (ROUGE-L). "Sup" denotes support score. "Entail" presents the percentage of outputs to which the entailment classifier predicts the entailment relation (built in Section 4.1). "Truthful" show the percentage of outputs to which a human subject judged as truthful headlines.

text: Conversely, we demonstrate that BPE inherently possesses the capacity to generate multiple segmentations for a single word. BPE constructs a subword vocabulary alongside a merge table, which delineates the specific subwords to be combined into larger units and establishes the precedence of these operations. In the segmentation phase, words are initially decomposed into character sequences; subsequently, the acquired merge operations are executed to aggregate these characters into increasingly larger, recognized symbols until no further merges are possible (Figure 1(a)). We propose BPE-dropout, a subword regularization technique that is both derived from and fully compatible with standard BPE. While it utilizes the vocabulary and merge table generated by BPE, it introduces stochasticity by randomly omitting certain merges at each step of the process. This stochastic approach yields diverse segmentations for the same word (Figure 1(b)). Our approach is characterized by its simplicity, as it necessitates no additional segmentation training beyond the standard BPE procedure and employs conventional BPE during inference. BPE-dropout demonstrates its effectiveness by outperforming both standard BPE and the method proposed by Kudo (2018) across a broad spectrum of translation tasks.
source_text: In contrast, we show that BPE itself incorporates the ability to produce multiple segmentations of the same word. BPE builds a vocabulary of subwords and a merge table, which specifies which subwords have to be merged into a bigger subword, as well as the priority of the merges. During segmentation, words are first split into sequences of characters, then the learned merge operations are applied to merge the characters into larger, known symbols, till no merge can be done (Figure 1(a) ). We introduce BPE-dropout -a subword regularization method based on and compatible with conventional BPE. It uses a vocabulary and a merge table built by BPE, but at each merge step, some merges are randomly dropped. This results in different segmentations for the same word (Figure 1(b) ). Our method requires no segmentation training in addition to BPE and uses standard BPE at test time, therefore is simple. BPE-dropout is superior compared to both BPE and Kudo (2018) on a wide range of translation tasks, therefore is effective.

text: Although conventional parsing methods typically depend on pre-trained aligners, several recent studies have explored alternative approaches. These include modeling alignments as latent variables (Lyu and Titov, 2018) and employing attention-based transduction frameworks that map sequences either to other sequences (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Konstas et al., 2017; van Noord and Bos, 2017) or directly to graphs (Cai and Lam, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b). Sequence-to-graph transduction models construct semantic graphs incrementally by instantiating a single node at each step. This property is advantageous for both computational efficiency and cognitive modeling, as it reflects the process by which human experts prioritize core concepts before elaborating on finer details (Banarescu et al., 2013; Cai and Lam, 2019).
source_text: While most previous works rely on a pre-trained aligner to train a parser, some recent attempts include: modeling the alignments as latent variables (Lyu and Titov, 2018) , attention-based sequenceto-sequence transduction models (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Konstas et al., 2017; van Noord and Bos, 2017) , and attention-based sequence-to-graph transduction models (Cai and Lam, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b) . Sequence-to-graph transduction models build a semantic graph incrementally via spanning one node at every step. This property is appealing in terms of both computational efficiency and cognitive modeling since it mimics what human experts usually do, i.e., first grasping the core ideas then digging into more details (Banarescu et al., 2013; Cai and Lam, 2019) .

text: Alternative approaches suggest assessing translation quality through the use of extrinsic proxy tasks. For instance, Berka et al. (2011) compiled brief English texts spanning multiple domains and formulated corresponding yes/no questions in Czech. After translating the source documents into Czech, they gauged machine translation (MT) system performance by measuring how effectively human participants could answer the questions using the translated material. Similarly, Scarton and Specia (2016) utilized various MT engines, including Google Translate and Bing Translate, to render German reading comprehension assessments into English, determining translation quality according to human scores on these translated tests. Regrettably, these extrinsic methodologies are plagued by the same limitations regarding scalability and consistency that characterize direct manual evaluation.
source_text: There are also some proposals to evaluate the quality of translations with the help of extrinsic proxy tasks. Berka et al. (2011) collected short English documents from various domains and created yes and no questions in Czech. They then translated the English documents into Czech and evaluated the quality of the MT systems based on human performances on the documents and questions in Czech. Scarton and Specia (2016) translated a dataset of German reading comprehension tests into English with various MT systems such as Google Translate and Bing Translate and judged the quality of translations based on human performances on the translated reading comprehension datasets. Unfortunately, these external tasks suffer from the same scalability and consistency issues as manual evaluation.
