text: In this section, we present the probing tasks utilized to assess the acquired sentence representations. Our methodology builds upon the framework and tasks established by [Citation], though our primary emphasis is on probing tasks pertinent to relation extraction (RE). Consequently, we incorporate several of their proposed tasks while developing novel probing tasks tailored specifically for RE. Consistent with their approach, the classification problem for these probing tasks relies exclusively on single sentence embeddings as input, rather than combinations of sentence and word embeddings or multiple sentence representations. This configuration aligns effectively with the conventional RE framework, which generally involves classifying the relation(s) between two entity mentions within a single sentence. Although our focus remains on supervised RE, this architecture is also relevant to distant supervision settings, where leading methods frequently feed sentence representations into a bag-level classifier to determine labels across all sentences associated with a specific entity pair (Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016). Following [Citation], we investigate various linguistic properties relevant to RE, spanning basic surface features (such as the distance between relation arguments), syntactic details (including parse tree depth and argument sequence), and semantic attributes (such as the entity types of the arguments). For both RE and probing experiments, we employ the standard training, validation, and test partitions of the original TACRED dataset. Regarding SemEval, we retain the original test set and allocate 10% of the training data for validation purposes. For TACRED, we utilize the provided named entity, part-of-speech, and dependency parse data, while SemEval is processed using the Stanford Parser (version 2018-10-05).
source_text: This section introduces the probing tasks we use to evaluate the learned sentence representations. We base our work on the setup and tasks introduced by , but focus on probing tasks related to relation extraction. We therefore adopt some of the tasks they propose, and introduce new probing tasks specifically designed for RE. As in their work, the probing task classification problem requires only single sentence embeddings as input (as opposed to, e.g., sentence and word embeddings, or multiple sentence representations). This fits the standard RE setup quite well, where the task is typically to classify the relation(s) expressed between a pair of entity mentions in a single sentence. While we focus on supervised relation extraction, this setup is also applicable in a distantly supervised RE setting, where state-of-the-art approaches are often based on passing sentence representations to a bag-level classifier that computes classification label(s) over all sentences for a given entity pair (Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016) . Similar to , we also aim to address a set of linguistic properties related to relation extraction ranging from simple surface phenomena (e.g. relation argument distance) to syntactic information (e.g. parse tree depth and argument ordering) and semantic information (e.g. the entity types of relation arguments). We use the standard training, validation, test split of the original TACRED dataset for RE and probing task experiments. For SemEval we reuse test and use 10% of the training set for validation. For TACRED we use the provided named entity, part-of-speech, and dependency parsing information, and parse SemEval with the Stanford Parser (2018-10-05 version) .

text: SyntaxGym facilitates the seamless construction and execution of controlled experiments regarding the syntactic proficiency of language models for linguists, while simultaneously enabling NLP researchers to benchmark their models against established standards. Upon submitting targeted syntactic evaluation suites to the platform, users receive automated assessments based on the language models integrated within the Gym. The platform further processes and graphically represents these evaluative outcomes. It serves to bridge the technical requirements of (a) deploying large-scale neural network language models with (b) the specialized linguistic expertise of researchers necessary for designing rigorous, theoretically substantive psycholinguistic experiments.
source_text: : SyntaxGym allows linguists to easily design and run controlled experiments on the syntactic knowledge of language models, and allows NLP experts to test their own models against these standards. Users submit targeted syntactic evaluation experiments to the site, and they are automatically evaluated on language models available in the Gym. SyntaxGym analyzes and visualizes these evaluation results. deploy large-scale neural network language models, and b) the linguistic knowledge of language scientists necessary to design controlled, theoretically interesting psycholinguistic experiments.

text: Logistic regression applied to bag-of-words features serves as the most effective baseline, surpassing histogram-based techniques. Although Gehrmann et al. (2019) achieved an AUC of 0.87 when classifying text as real or generated by applying logistic regression to the four GLTR system buckets, we observed an AUC between 0.52 and 0.56 for the same task. This variation likely results from the fact that our discriminator’s human-written training data shares the same distribution as the text used to train the language model, while the human-authored text in GLTR is sourced from children’s literature, scientific abstracts, and news reports. Selecting appropriate training data is vital for learned detection systems; for real-world use, the data should correspond to the genres that generation systems are designed to emulate. Regarding the fine-tuned BERT model, Figure 1a depicts discriminator accuracy relative to excerpt length and sampling method. As might be expected, accuracy correlates positively with sequence length. For unconditioned text produced via nucleus sampling ($p=0.96$) and untruncated random sampling ($p=1.0$), accuracy increases from a near-chance 55% to roughly 81% for the longest tested sequences. Conversely, discriminators trained and tested on top-$k$ sampling exceed 80% accuracy even on excerpts of only 16 tokens.
source_text: Logistic regression on bag-of-words is the best of the baselines, beating out the histogram-based methods. While Gehrmann et al. (2019) report an AUC of 0.87 on classifying text as real or generated using logistic regression on the four buckets of the GLTR system, we report AUC between 0.52 and 0.56 for this task. The discrepancy is likely due to the fact that the human-written text in our discriminator training set comes from the same distribution as the text used to train the language model, while in GLTR the human text comes from children's books, scientific abstracts, and newspaper articles. The selection of training data for learned detection systems is crucial. In real-world applications, the choice ought to reflect the genres that builders of text-generation systems are trying to impersonate. Fine-tuned BERT In Figure 1a , we begin by observing discriminator accuracy as a function of excerpt length and sampling method. As can be intuitively expected, as sequence length increases, so too does accuracy. For unconditioned text decoded with nucleus (p0.96) and untruncated (p1.0) random sampling, we find discriminator accuracy increases from 55%, near random, to about 81% for the longest sequences tested. In contrast, discriminators trained and evaluated on top-k achieve over 80% accuracy even on 16-token excerpts.

text: Various strategies have been introduced to mitigate performance degradation, including (1) knowledge distillation (Oord et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2019a,b) and (2) the imposition of source-target alignment constraints through fertility (Gu et al., 2017), word mapping (Guo et al., 2019a), attention distillation (Li et al., 2019b), and duration prediction. While these techniques enable non-autoregressive (NAR) models to achieve parity with autoregressive (AR) models in certain contexts, a performance gap remains evident in others (Gu et al., 2017). Consequently, several questions naturally arise: (1) Why does this disparity persist for specific tasks, and are some tasks inherently more challenging for NAR generation than others? (2) Why do methods such as knowledge distillation and source-target alignment effectively facilitate NAR generation?
source_text: Several techniques have been proposed to alleviate the accuracy degradation, including 1) knowledge distillation (Oord et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2019a,b; , 2) imposing source-target alignment constraint with fertility (Gu et al., 2017) , word mapping (Guo et al., 2019a) , attention distillation (Li et al., 2019b) and duration prediction . With the help of those techniques, it is observed that NAR models can match the accuracy of AR models for some tasks , but the gap still exists for some other tasks (Gu et al., 2017; . Therefore, several questions come out naturally: (1) Why the gap still exists for some tasks? Are some tasks more difficult for NAR generation than others? (2) Why the techniques like knowledge distillation and source-target alignment can help NAR generation?

text: Following Escolano et al. (2019), we implement three types of training objectives in our model. (i) Translation objective: While bilingual NMT models typically utilize cross-entropy loss as the training objective (denoted as $L_{s2t}$), we also incorporate an additional loss, $L_{t2s}$, for translation from the target back to the source. Furthermore, consistent with 2018, we include a reconstruction loss to minimize information loss. We define the reconstruction of $X$ as $\hat{X} = \text{Decoder}(\text{Interlingua}(\text{Encoder}(X)))$. Accordingly, we employ the cross-entropy between $X$ and $\hat{X}$ as our reconstruction loss, denoted as $L_{s2s}$ for the source and $L_{t2t}$ for the target.
source_text: We introduce three types of training objectives in our model, similar to (Escolano et al., 2019) . (i) Translation objective: Generally, a bilingual NMT model adopts the cross-entropy loss as the training objective, which we denote as L s2t , meanwhile, we incorporate another loss L t2s for translation from the target to the source. 2018, we incorporate an reconstruction loss for the purpose of minimizing information loss. We denote the X = Decoder(Interlingua(Encoder(X))) as the reconstruction of X. So we employ crossentropy between X and X as our reconstruction loss, and denote L s2s for the source, L t2t for the target.

text: Contextual models integrate information from the text itself alongside the circumstances of its disclosure, such as author-specific data. Research in this area is currently limited. Utilizing Twitter data, Bamman and Smith (2015a) represented author context through manually engineered features derived from historical posts. Amir et al. (2016) aggregated all past tweets into a single document and employed the Paragraph Vector model (Le and Mikolov, 2014) to generate a corresponding document embedding. Building upon this, Hazarika et al. (2018) extracted further personality-based features from consolidated historical tweets using a model pre-trained on a personality detection corpus. Following a similar strategy, Oprea and Magdy (2019) developed individual embeddings for each historical tweet and defined author context as their weighted average. While these previous models report promising results, they were trained and evaluated on datasets annotated through manual labeling, distant supervision, or a hybrid of both. We argue that these labeling methodologies are limited in their capacity to capture sarcasm as intended by the authors without introducing noise. The following section details how such noise can manifest.
source_text: Contextual models These models utilize information from both the text and the context of its disclosure, such as author information. There is a limited amount of work in this direction. Using Twitter data, Bamman and Smith (2015a) represent author context as manually-curated features extracted from their historical tweets. Amir et al. (2016) merge all historical tweets into one document and use the Paragraph Vector model (Le and Mikolov, 2014) to build an embedding of that document. Building on this, Hazarika et al. 2018extract additional personality features from the merged historical tweets with a model pre-trained on a personality detection corpus. Using the same strategy, Oprea and Magdy (2019) build separate embeddings for each historical tweet and identify author context with their the weighted average. Despite reporting encouraging results, all previous models are trained and tested on datasets annotated via manual labelling, distant supervision, or a mix between them. We believe both labelling meth-ods are limited in their ability to capture sarcasm in texts as intended by the authors of the texts without noise. We now discuss how noise can occur.

text: A primary challenge in style transfer (ST) involves the constraints parallel sentences impose on automatic evaluation metrics. On one hand, because parallel sentences are presumed to be semantically equivalent, many datasets exhibit significant string overlap between aligned pairs, resulting in elevated BLEU scores. Conversely, these sentence pairs ought to manifest distinct styles and varied expressions, which should theoretically yield lower BLEU scores. Consequently, developing a test dataset that effectively balances these two requirements is of critical importance. We evaluate the quality of the test sentence pairs across various datasets, following the methodology of Fu et al. (2019). Increased BLEU scores indicate greater similarity between sentences, whereas higher edit distances (ED) signify more heterogeneous structures. Table 3 details the BLEU and ED metrics. It should be noted that the semantic equivalence of each parallel sentence pair was verified during the annotation process. Our observations indicate that: (1) the MSD dataset exhibits the lowest BLEU and highest ED scores, suggesting it is a particularly demanding task necessitating both lexical and structural transformations; and (2) Text Simplification (TS) datasets demonstrate more pronounced structural disparities—evidenced by higher ED values—than ST datasets, implying that the inherent nature of language complexity makes TS a more difficult transfer task.
source_text: One of the main concerns in ST is the limitations of parallel sentences towards automatic evaluation. On one hand, assuming that the parallel sentences have the same meaning, many datasets find the aligned sentences to have higher string overlap (as measured by BLEU). On the other hand, the two sentences should have different styles, and may vary a lot in expressions: and thus leading to a lower BLEU. Hence how to build a testing dataset that considers both criteria is critical. We analyze the quality of testing sentence pairs in each dataset. (Fu et al., 2019) . Higher BLEUs imply two more similar sentences, while higher edit distances imply more heterogeneous structures. Table 3 presents the BLEU and edit distance (ED for short) scores. Note that each pair of parallel sentences is verified to convey the same meaning during annotation. We see that: (1) MSD has the lowest BLEU and highest ED. This implies that MSD is very challenging that requires both lexical and structural modifications. (2) TS datasets reflect more structural differences (with higher ED values) as compared to ST datasets. This means that TS datasets concerning the nature of language complexity (simplicity) are more complex to transfer.

text: The edit and generation models share identical parameters, which were tuned using the validation set. The encoders feature a hidden dimension of 64, whereas the decoder utilizes a dimension of 128, and both code and comment embeddings are set to 64. For the edit model, embeddings are initialized with pre-trained vectors from the generation model. Training is conducted using the Adam optimizer with a dropout rate of 0.6, a batch size of 100, and an initial learning rate of 0.001. The models are optimized to minimize negative log-likelihood, with training terminating if the validation loss fails to decrease for ten consecutive epochs. During the inference phase, we employ beam search with a beam width of 20.
source_text: Model parameters are identical across the edit model and generation model, tuned on validation data. Encoders have hidden dimension 64, the decoder has hidden dimension 128, and the dimension for code and comment embeddings is 64. The embeddings used in the edit model are initialized using the pre-trained embedding vectors from the generation model. We use a dropout rate of 0.6, a batch size of 100, an initial learning rate of 0.001, and Adam optimizer. Models are trained to minimize negative log likelihood, and we terminate training if the validation loss does not decrease for ten consecutive epochs. During inference, we use beam search with beam width=20.

text: Upon receiving natural language input, our module expands the training dataset by performing soft-matching between unlabeled sentences and logical forms parsed from natural language explanations. In its handling of natural language, the module implements the Neural Execution Tree framework, where a semantic parser first converts explanations into logical forms. While previous studies have employed similar logical forms to enhance model training via strict matching against unlabeled sentence pools, our approach introduces a refined training paradigm that relaxes these constraints. This relaxation improves weak labeling coverage and permits the inclusion of a more extensive set of unlabeled data. The module assumes that each natural language explanation can be decomposed into a logical form consisting of clauses with predicates from four specific categories—a design choice that informs the UI's autosuggest feature. During the weak labeling phase, the module calculates the likelihood that an unlabeled sentence aligns with each clause, producing an aggregate score for the match. If this score surpasses configurable thresholds, the sentence is assigned a weak label accordingly.
source_text: When natural language is inputted into the system, our module grows training data via soft-matching between logical forms parsed from natural language explanations and unlabeled sentences. The module follows the Neural Execution Tree framework of when dealing with natural language. First, the explanation is parsed into a logical form by a semantic parser. Previous works have suggested using similar logical forms to improve model training by strict matching on the pool of unlabeled sentences to generate additional labeled data. However, proposes an improved model training paradigm, which relaxes this strict matching constraint, subsequently improving weak labeling coverage and allowing for a larger pool of unlabeled data to be used for model training. Our module does assume each NL explanation can be broken down into a logical form composed of clauses consisting of predicates from four categories--hence the autosuggest feature in the UI. At weak labeling, the module scores how likely a given unlabeled sentence fits each clause and then constructs an aggregate score representing the match between the logical form and the unlabeled sentence. If the final score is above configurable thresholds, we weakly label the sentence with the appropriate label.

text: Rather than proposing a novel deep learning toolkit for NLP, we leverage established frameworks (HuggingFace, 2019; Paszke et al., 2019; Gardner et al., 2018) and external API services, specifically TagME and Bing Web Search via Azure Cognitive Service. Similarly, the majority of tasks utilize existing algorithms: language modeling (Radford et al., 2019), knowledge-grounded generation (Qin et al., 2019; Prabhumoye et al., 2019) or span retrieval (Seo et al., 2016; Devlin et al., 2018), style transfer (Gao et al., 2019c,b), and constrained generation (Hokamp and Liu, 2017).
source_text: As the goal is not another deep learning NLP toolkit, we rely on existing ones (HuggingFace, 2019; Paszke et al., 2019; Gardner et al., 2018) and online API services Bing Web Search provided in Azure Cognitive Service 3 and TagME. 4 Similarly, most tasks are using existing algorithms: language modeling Radford et al., 2019) , knowledge grounded generation (Qin et al., 2019; Prabhumoye et al., 2019) or span retrieval (Seo et al., 2016; Devlin et al., 2018) , style transfer (Gao et al., 2019c,b) and constrained generation (Hokamp and Liu, 2017) .

text: This study investigates the resolution of bridging anaphora, a process in which anaphors and their antecedents are connected via diverse frame-based, lexico-semantic, or encyclopedic associations. Adopting the approach of Hou et al. (2013b), we primarily address "referential bridging," a category where the expressions are genuinely anaphoric and the bridging links are context-dependent. As shown in Example 1, lexical semantics alone may suggest that both "her building" and "buildings with substantial damage" are viable antecedent candidates for the anaphor "residents." To identify the correct antecedent—buildings with substantial damage—it is necessary to account for the semantics of the wider discourse context.
source_text: In this paper, we focus on bridging anaphora resolution where bridging anaphors and their antecedents are linked via various lexico-semantic, frame or encyclopedic relations. Following Hou et al. (2013b) and , we mainly consider "referential bridging" in which bridging anaphors are truly anaphoric and bridging relations are context-dependent. In Example 1 1 , both "her building" and "buildings with substantial damage" are plausible antecedent candidates for the bridging anaphor "residents" based on lexical semantics. In order to find the antecedent (buildings with substantial damage), we have to take the meaning of the broader discourse context into account.

text: We obtain source sentences, system outputs, and reference translations from the WMT17–19 news translation shared tasks (Bojar et al., 2017b; Ma et al., 2018b, 2019). These tasks provide predictions from 166 translation systems across 16 language pairs in WMT17, 149 systems across 14 pairs in WMT18, and 233 systems across 18 pairs in WMT19. Our evaluation targets X-to-English (X-en) pairs, where X represents a diverse group of 12 languages: German (de), Chinese (zh), Czech (cs), Latvian (lv), Finnish (fi), Russian (ru), Turkish (tr), Gujarati (gu), Kazakh (kk), Lithuanian (lt), and Estonian (et). Each language pair within the WMT17–19 datasets comprises approximately 3,000 source sentences, with each sentence linked to a single reference translation and the automatic translations produced by the participating systems.
source_text: We collect the source language sentences, their system and reference translations from the WMT17-19 news translation shared task (Bojar et al., 2017b; Ma et al., 2018b Ma et al., , 2019 , which contains predictions of 166 translation systems across 16 language pairs in WMT17, 149 translation systems across 14 language pairs in WMT18 and 233 translation systems across 18 language pairs in WMT19. We evaluate for X-en language pairs, selecting X from a set of 12 diverse languages: German (de), Chinese (zh), Czech (cs), Latvian (lv), Finnish (fi), Russian (ru), and Turkish (tr), Gujarati (gu), Kazakh (kk), Lithuanian (lt) and Estonian (et). Each language pair in WMT17-19 has approximately 3,000 source sentences, each associated to one reference translation and to the automatic translations generated by participating systems.

text: This preliminary study compares SOCO-QA against the traditional BM25 model (Robertson et al., 2009). BM25-based techniques remain the standard for document retrieval within industrial applications. Prior work in open-domain question answering has indicated that BM25 is a formidable baseline to surpass, especially when questions are formulated by contributors with prior knowledge of the answers (Lee et al., 2019a). We leave more exhaustive comparisons with other learning-based methods to future research, as the primary objective of this demonstration paper is to present the system and its dataset. We employ the integrated Elasticsearch (Gormley and Tong, 2015) alongside the Natural Questions (NQ), MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016), and Trivia QA (Joshi et al., 2017) datasets. Documents from the development set are segmented into sentence-level answer frames, and development queries are used to compute Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) and Recall at 5 (R@5) as evaluation metrics. The data statistics are summarized in Table 2.
source_text: This preliminary studies focuses on comparison between SOCO-QA against classic BM25 (Robertson et al., 2009) . BM25-based methods remain to be the mainstream methods for document retrieval in industry. Previous work in open domain question answering has shown that BM25 is a difficult baseline to surpass when questions were written by workers who have prior knowledge of the answer (Lee et al., 2019a) . We will leave more comprehensive comparisons against other learning-based methods to future work, since the main goal of this demo paper is to present the system along with its dataset. We use the built-in elastic search (Gormley and Tong, 2015) (NQ) , MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016) and Trivia QA (Joshi et al., 2017) . We break documents from the development set into sentence-level answer frames, and uses the queries in the development set to compute Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) and Recall at 5 (R@5) as the evaluation metrics. The data statics are summarized in Table 2 .

text: Table 1 presents the performance of word-by-word translation systems for the language pairs fr ↔ en, ru ↔ en, and zh ↔ en. In the absence of cross-lingual pre-training, word-by-word translation using an artificial dictionary yields scores of 7.76 (fr→en), 4.88 (en→fr), 3.05 (ru→en), 1.60 (en→ru), 1.99 (zh→en), and 1.14 (en→zh). Utilizing a ground-truth dictionary results in marginal improvements, with scores of 7.97, 6.61, 4.17, 2.81, 2.68, and 1.79, respectively. By contrast, the UNMT system (Lample et al., 2018b) achieves a score of 24. These results demonstrate that while the ground-truth dictionary slightly outperforms the artificial lexicon generated by Conneau et al. (2018), both methods perform poorly. This indicates that rudimentary word-by-word substitution is inadequate as a translation method, necessitating more effective strategies for utilizing bilingual dictionaries to enhance performance.
source_text: Results of Word-by-word Translation system fr → en en → fr ru → en en → ru zh → en en → zh Without Cross-lingual Pre-training Word-by-word using artificial dictionary 7.76 4.88 3.05 1.60 1.99 1.14 Word-by-word using ground-truth dictionary 7.97 6.61 4.17 2.81 2.68 1.79 UNMT (Lample et al., 2018b) 24 It shows that using the ground-truth dictionary is slightly better than using the artificial one generated by Conneau et al. (2018) . Both performances are remarkably bad, indicating that simple word-byword translation is not qualified as an MT method. More effective utilization of the bilingual dictionary is needed to improve the translation performance.

text: Ideally, a summarization system would yield a ROUGE score of 100, signifying that the generated output is indistinguishable from the reference. Nevertheless, such a target is impractical given the constraints of the Gigaword dataset. Summarization is fundamentally an underconstrained problem, as the salience of information is largely dictated by the reader's prior knowledge and specific expectations (Kryściński et al., 2019). Moreover, Gigaword—and similar standard benchmarks—contains inherent noise for summarization research, having been originally compiled for professional news distribution rather than experimental evaluation. Consequently, current state-of-the-art approaches are limited to ROUGE-1 scores below 40 on this corpus.
source_text: Apparently, a summarization method is desirable to achieve a ROUGE score of 100, i.e., a system output is identical to the reference. However, this is an unrealistic goal for the task setting on the Gigaword dataset. The summarization task is underconstrained in that the importance of a piece of information highly depends on the expectations and prior knowledge of a reader (Kryściński et al., 2019) . In addition, the Gigaword dataset (as well as other widely-used datasets) was noisy for summarization research because it was not created for the research objective but other professional activities (e.g., news production and distribution). Thus, the state-of-the-art method could only reach ROUGE-1 scores less than 40 on the dataset.

text: As a user commences the essay-writing process, the system provides prompts featuring pertinent grammatical patterns, collocations, and bilingual examples. These real-time writing suggestions are dynamically updated based on the most recently entered word or phrase. Furthermore, users can retrieve specific lexical information by hovering over a given word. For example, Section A of Figure 1 (bottom left) displays the suggestions generated for "finish." Once the writing is complete, clicking the "Check" button activates the subsequent components.
source_text: When a user begins to write an essay, the system responds with prompts of related grammar patterns, collocations, and bilingual examples. These continuous writing suggestions are based on the last word or phrase the user has entered. Additionally, the user can get information of a certain word by mousing over it. For example, suggestions for "finish" are shown in Section A of Figure 1 (bottom left). Once finishing the writings, the user can click the Check button triggering the following components.

text: While statistical tests applied to large volumes of noisy ratings can distinguish between dialogue systems, reliably identifying specific low-performing dialogues to facilitate system improvement remains difficult. In this paper, we address the challenge of calibrating extensive, noisy, self-reported user ratings to construct superior dialogue evaluation models. We frame this task as a two-step process: first denoising the self-reported ratings and then training a model on the refined data. To this end, we propose CMADE (Comparison Model for Automatic Dialogue Evaluation), a progressive three-stage denoising pipeline. The process begins with self-supervised learning to derive robust dialogue representations. We then fine-tune CMADE on smoothed self-reported ratings to enhance these representations while preventing the network from overfitting to noise. Finally, we utilize Data Shapley to eliminate noisy training instances and fine-tune the model on the cleaned dataset. Our experiments indicate that CMADE successfully identifies noisy data, achieving 89.2% accuracy and a Kappa coefficient of 0.787 on a test set of unseen, expert-rated dialogue pairs.
source_text: Although we could tell which dialog system is better by running statistical test on a large number of noisy ratings, it is difficult to locate dialogs with bad performance reliably to improve dialog system quality. In this paper, we take on the challenge of calibrating a large number of noisy self-reported user ratings to build better dialog evaluation models. We formulate the task as to first denoise the self-reported user ratings and then train a model on the cleaned ratings. We design CMADE (Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation), a progressive three-stage denoising pipeline. We first perform a self-supervised learning to obtain good dialog representations. We then fine-tune CMADE on smoothed self-reported user ratings to improve the dialog representation while preventing the network from overfitting on noisy ratings. Finally, we apply data Shapley to remove noisy training data, and fine-tune the model on the cleaned training set. Our experiments show that CMADE is able to successfully identify noisy training data and achieves 89.2% in accuracy and 0.787 in Kappa on a test set with unseen expert-rated dialog pairs.

text: As illustrated in Figure 2, our document-level graph incorporates three distinct node categories: mention nodes, entity nodes, and meta dependency path (MDP) nodes. Mention nodes represent the various entity occurrences within each sentence, while entity node representations are derived by averaging their constituent mentions. To construct document-level graphs, existing methodologies typically utilize every node within a sentence's dependency tree (Sahu et al., 2019) or employ a single sentence-level node formed by averaging all token embeddings. In contrast, we leverage tokens situated along the shortest dependency path between mentions within a sentence. This approach is widely adopted in sentence-level relation extraction for its capacity to isolate salient information while discarding irrelevant noise (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Xu et al., 2015a,b). Unlike standard sentence-level extraction, which generally assumes only two entities per sentence, our framework accounts for the multiple mentions that may appear within a single sentence.
source_text: We construct three types of nodes for a documentlevel graph: mention nodes, entity nodes and meta dependency paths (MDP) nodes as shown in Figure 2. Mention nodes correspond to different mentions of entities in each sentence. The representation of an entity node is computed as the average of its mentions. To build a document-level graph, existing approaches use all nodes in the dependency tree of a sentence (Sahu et al., 2019) or one sentence-level node by averaging all token representations of the sentence . Alternatively, we use tokens on the shortest dependency path between mentions in the sentence. The shortest dependency path has been widely used in the sentence-level relation extraction as it is able to effectively make use of relevant information while ignoring irrelevant information (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Xu et al., 2015a,b) . Unlike sentence-level extraction, where each sentence only has two entities, each sentence here may involve multiple mentions.

text: This section details the architectural design of PHOTON. While we are iteratively refining the neural semantic parser's performance, the current implementation focuses on processing standalone queries, with the integration of interaction context reserved for future work. The comprehensive system workflow is illustrated in Figure 1. PHOTON is an end-to-end framework that accepts a natural language question and a database schema as input, ultimately returning query results by executing synthesized SQL code. Designed for practical industrial applications, the system employs a modular architecture centered on two primary components: a neural SQL parser and a confusion detection mechanism. The SQL parser leverages an encoder-decoder framework to map the input question and schema into an executable SQL query. Meanwhile, the confusion detection module identifies untranslatable queries and isolates the specific spans responsible for the failure. These problematic tokens, along with their context, are then passed to an autocorrection module to predict the user's intended question.
source_text: In this section, we will elaborate on the system design of PHOTON. 1 We are continuously improving the performance of the neural semantic parser. Currently the semantic parser only accepts standalone question as input. We plan to also model the interaction context in future work. Figure 1 shows the overall workflow of our system. PHOTON is an end-to-end system that takes a user question and database schema as input, and output the query result after executing the generated SQL on the database. PHOTON is a modular framework designed towards practical industrial applications. The core modules in PHOTON are the SQL parser and confusion detection mechanism. The SQL parser parses the input question and database schema, maps them into executable SQL query via an encoder-decoder framework. The confusion detection module identifies the untranslatable questions and captures the confusing span of the untranslatable question. The confusing tokens together with the context are fed into the autocorrection module to make a prediction of user attempted question.
